\newcommand{\Aut}{\operatorname{Aut}}

\title{Original Problem: Computably \(\operatorname{AUT}\)-Countable Structures on a Cone and Non-\(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\)-Definable Automorphisms}
\author{}
\date{}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\section*{Overview / Strategy}

We build a computable structure in a finite relational language with three sorts.  The first sort is a rigid successor ray of labels.  The second sort codes finite subsets of the label set, with a relation \(Q\) that toggles one label at a time.  The third sort places, above each label, two directed successor rays.  The finite subset coded on the second sort determines which pairs of rays are swapped.  Sections~\ref{sec:construction} and~\ref{sec:automorphisms} give the construction and prove that the automorphisms are exactly the finite toggles.

The copy-computability argument is nonuniform in the automorphism, as required by Turing reducibility: for a given copy and a given automorphism, the finite support and finitely many roots can be hardwired into an oracle program using the atomic diagram of the copy.  This is proved in Section~\ref{sec:copies}.  Finally, Section~\ref{sec:nondef} proves nondefinability.  Given any finite parameter tuple, we choose a fresh two-ray gadget.  The automorphism swaps the two rays of that gadget, while an injective shift of one ray preserves and reflects all atomic facts and fixes the parameters.  Existential formulas true of the genuine graph pair therefore remain true of a false pair, ruling out any \(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\)-definition of that automorphism from the allowed parameters.

\section{Definitions and the headline theorem}

Throughout, \(\omega=\mathbb N\).  A copy of a countable structure means a structure with domain \(\omega\).  If \(\mathcal B\) is a structure in a finite relational language, \(D(\mathcal B)\) denotes its complete atomic diagram; equivalently, up to Turing equivalence, it is the finite join of the characteristic functions of the basic relations of \(\mathcal B\).

\begin{definition}
Let \(\mathcal M\) be a countable structure with domain \(\omega\).
\begin{enumerate}
\item \(\mathcal M\) is \emph{computably \(\operatorname{AUT}\)-countable} if every \(\rho\in\Aut(\mathcal M)\) satisfies \(\rho\leq_T D(\mathcal M)\).
\item \(\mathcal M\) is \emph{computably \(\operatorname{AUT}\)-countable on a cone} if there is \(C\subseteq\omega\) such that for every copy \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal M\) with domain \(\omega\), every \(\rho\in\Aut(\mathcal B)\) satisfies \(\rho\leq_T D(\mathcal B)\oplus C\), where \(\oplus\) is Turing join.
\end{enumerate}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}
An existential formula is a formula of the form
\[
\exists z_0\cdots \exists z_{n-1}\,\theta,
\]
where \(n<\omega\) and \(\theta\) is quantifier-free.  In the final nondefinability argument we prove the stronger statement allowing \(\theta\) to be a quantifier-free \(L_{\omega_1\omega}\)-formula built from atomic formulas by negation and countable conjunctions and disjunctions.

Let \(P\subseteq M\) be finite.  A function \(f:M\to M\) is \emph{\(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\)-definable in \(\mathcal M\) over parameters from \(P\)} if there is a countable family \((\varphi_i(x,y))_{i\in\omega}\) of existential formulas, each using only parameters from \(P\), such that for all \(x,y\in M\),
\[
f(x)=y
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal M\models \varphi_i(x,y)\text{ for some }i\in\omega.
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{theorem}[Headline theorem: positive answer to the original problem]\label{thm:main}
There is a computable structure \(\mathcal A\) with domain \(\omega\), in a finite relational language, such that:
\begin{enumerate}
\item For every copy \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\) with domain \(\omega\), every automorphism of \(\mathcal B\) is computable relative to \(D(\mathcal B)\).  Thus \(\mathcal A\) is computably \(\operatorname{AUT}\)-countable on a cone with cone base \(\emptyset\).
\item For every finite tuple \(\bar a\) from \(\mathcal A\), there is an automorphism \(\pi\in\Aut(\mathcal A)\) such that \(\pi\) is not \(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\)-definable in \(\mathcal A\) over parameters from
\[
\rng(\bar a)\cup \pi[\rng(\bar a)].
\]
\end{enumerate}
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
The structure is constructed and proved computable in Proposition~\ref{prop:construction}.  Proposition~\ref{prop:copies} proves clause~(1).  Proposition~\ref{prop:nondef} proves clause~(2), indeed with quantifier-free infinitary matrices allowed in the existential formulas.  These propositions concern the same structure \(\mathcal A\), so together they prove the theorem.
\end{proof}

\section{Construction of the computable structure}\label{sec:construction}

Fix a computable pairing bijection \(\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle:\omega^2\to\omega\) with computable projections.  For \(e\in\omega\), let
\[
F_e=\{i\in\omega:\text{the \(i\)-th binary digit of \(e\) is \(1\)}\}.
\]
Thus \(F_e\) is finite, and every finite \(F\subseteq\omega\) has the unique code
\[
c(F)=\sum_{i\in F}2^i
\]
with \(F_{c(F)}=F\).

For \(l,e,k\in\omega\) and \(b\in\{0,1\}\), put
\[
\lambda_l=3l,\qquad u_e=3e+1,\qquad v_{l,b,k}=3\langle l,2k+b\rangle+2.
\]
The language is the finite relational language
\[
\tau=\{L,U,V,N,I,Q,S,T\},
\]
where \(L,U,V\) are unary, \(N,I\) are binary, and \(Q,S,T\) are ternary.

Define a \(\tau\)-structure \(\mathcal A\) with domain \(\omega\) as follows.  The sort predicates are
\[
L^{\mathcal A}=\{\lambda_l:l\in\omega\},\quad
U^{\mathcal A}=\{u_e:e\in\omega\},\quad
V^{\mathcal A}=\{v_{l,b,k}:l,k\in\omega,\ b\in\{0,1\}\}.
\]
The remaining relations hold exactly in the following displayed cases:
\[
N(\lambda_l,\lambda_m)\Longleftrightarrow m=l+1,
\]
\[
I(\lambda_l,v_{m,b,k})\Longleftrightarrow l=m,
\]
\[
Q(\lambda_l,u_e,u_{e'})\Longleftrightarrow F_{e'}=F_e\triangle\{l\},
\]
\[
S(\lambda_l,v_{l,b,k},v_{l,b,k+1})
\quad\text{for all }l,b,k,
\]
and
\[
T(\lambda_l,u_e,v_{m,b,k})
\Longleftrightarrow
m=l\text{ and }b=\chi_{F_e}(l),
\]
where \(\triangle\) is symmetric difference and \(\chi_F\) is the characteristic function of \(F\).  For later use, write
\[
R_{l,b}=\{v_{l,b,k}:k\in\omega\},
\qquad
P_l=R_{l,0}\cup R_{l,1}.
\]

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:construction}
The structure \(\mathcal A\) is a computable structure with domain \(\omega\) in the finite relational language \(\tau\).  Moreover, \(L^{\mathcal A}\), \(U^{\mathcal A}\), and \(V^{\mathcal A}\) partition \(\omega\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
The three displayed sets lie in the three different congruence classes modulo \(3\), so they are pairwise disjoint.  If \(n\equiv 0\pmod 3\), then \(n=\lambda_{n/3}\).  If \(n\equiv 1\pmod 3\), then \(n=u_{(n-1)/3}\).  If \(n\equiv 2\pmod 3\), let \(q=(n-2)/3\).  The computable projections of the pairing function give unique \(l,t\) with \(q=\langle l,t\rangle\), and \(t\) has a unique form \(2k+b\) with \(b\in\{0,1\}\); hence \(n=v_{l,b,k}\).  Thus the three sorts partition \(\omega\), and membership in each sort is computable.

The relations are decidable from the displayed coordinates.  For \(N\), one checks that both arguments are labels and then compares their indices.  For \(I\), one checks that the first argument is a label and the second is a \(V\)-element and compares the label coordinates.  For \(Q\), one checks that the arguments have forms \(\lambda_l,u_e,u_{e'}\) and then decides whether the binary digits of \(e'\) are obtained from those of \(e\) by flipping exactly the \(l\)-th bit.  For \(S\), one checks that the arguments have the form \(\lambda_l,v_{l,b,k},v_{l,b,j}\) and then tests \(j=k+1\).  For \(T\), one checks that the arguments have the form \(\lambda_l,u_e,v_{m,b,k}\) and then tests \(m=l\) and whether the \(l\)-th binary digit of \(e\) is \(b\).  Hence every basic relation is computable, so \(\mathcal A\) is computable.
\end{proof}

\section{Classification of automorphisms}\label{sec:automorphisms}

For a finite \(F\subseteq\omega\), define \(\alpha_F:\omega\to\omega\) by
\[
\alpha_F(\lambda_l)=\lambda_l,\qquad
\alpha_F(u_e)=u_{c(F_e\triangle F)},\qquad
\alpha_F(v_{l,b,k})=v_{l,b\oplus\chi_F(l),k},
\]
where \(\oplus\) is addition modulo \(2\).

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:alpha-auto}
For every finite \(F\subseteq\omega\), \(\alpha_F\in\Aut(\mathcal A)\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The map \(\alpha_F\) is an involution: toggling \(F\) twice restores each \(U\)-element, and adding \(\chi_F(l)\) modulo \(2\) twice restores each side of each \(V\)-gadget.  Hence it is bijective.  It preserves \(L,U,V\) setwise.  It fixes all labels, so it preserves \(N\).  It does not change the label coordinate of any \(V\)-element, so it preserves \(I\).

For \(S\), if \(S(\lambda_l,v_{l,b,k},v_{l,b,k+1})\) holds, then after applying \(\alpha_F\) we get
\[
S(\lambda_l,v_{l,b\oplus\chi_F(l),k},v_{l,b\oplus\chi_F(l),k+1}),
\]
which holds.  For \(Q\), if \(Q(\lambda_l,u_e,u_{e'})\) holds, then
\[
F_{e'}=F_e\triangle\{l\}.
\]
The two images in the \(U\)-sort code \(F_e\triangle F\) and \(F_{e'}\triangle F\), and
\[
F_{e'}\triangle F=(F_e\triangle\{l\})\triangle F=(F_e\triangle F)\triangle\{l\},
\]
so \(Q\) is preserved.  For \(T\), if \(T(\lambda_l,u_e,v_{m,b,k})\) holds, then \(m=l\) and \(b=\chi_{F_e}(l)\).  The image of \(u_e\) codes \(F_e\triangle F\), and the image side is
\[
b\oplus\chi_F(l)=\chi_{F_e}(l)\oplus\chi_F(l)=\chi_{F_e\triangle F}(l),
\]
so \(T\) is preserved.  Since \(\alpha_F\) is an involution, preservation of each relation implies reflection as well.  Thus \(\alpha_F\) is an automorphism.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:all-aut}
Every automorphism of \(\mathcal A\) is \(\alpha_F\) for a unique finite \(F\subseteq\omega\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(\beta\in\Aut(\mathcal A)\).  First, \(\beta\) fixes every label.  The element \(\lambda_0\) is the unique \(L\)-element with no \(N\)-predecessor, so \(\beta(\lambda_0)=\lambda_0\).  If \(\beta(\lambda_l)=\lambda_l\), then \(\lambda_{l+1}\) is the unique \(N\)-successor of \(\lambda_l\), so \(\beta(\lambda_{l+1})=\lambda_{l+1}\).  Induction gives \(\beta(\lambda_l)=\lambda_l\) for all \(l\).

For \(l\in\omega\), let \(\tau_l:U^{\mathcal A}\to U^{\mathcal A}\) be the toggle map
\[
\tau_l(u_e)=u_{c(F_e\triangle\{l\})}.
\]
For every \(u\in U^{\mathcal A}\), \(Q(\lambda_l,u,\tau_l(u))\) holds, and the third entry satisfying this formula is unique.  Since \(\beta\) fixes \(\lambda_l\) and preserves \(Q\),
\[
\beta(\tau_l(u))=\tau_l(\beta(u)).
\]
Let \(\beta(u_0)=u_d\) and put \(F=F_d\).  If \(F_e=\{l_0,\dots,l_{n-1}\}\), then
\[
u_e=\tau_{l_0}\cdots\tau_{l_{n-1}}(u_0).
\]
Using the commutation relation above,
\[
\beta(u_e)
=\tau_{l_0}\cdots\tau_{l_{n-1}}(u_d)
=u_{c(F_e\triangle F)}.
\]

It remains to analyze the \(V\)-sort.  Because \(\beta\) fixes each \(\lambda_l\) and preserves \(I\), it maps \(P_l\) onto itself.  On \(P_l\), the binary relation \(S(\lambda_l,\cdot,\cdot)\) is exactly the disjoint union of the two directed rays
\[
v_{l,b,0}\to v_{l,b,1}\to v_{l,b,2}\to\cdots
\qquad (b=0,1).
\]
The two roots \(v_{l,0,0}\) and \(v_{l,1,0}\) are exactly the elements of \(P_l\) with no \(S(\lambda_l,\cdot,\cdot)\)-predecessor.  Thus \(\beta\) either fixes the two roots or swaps them.  Since every element has a unique successor on its ray, the image of a root determines the image of the whole ray.  Hence for each \(l\) there is \(\epsilon_l\in\{0,1\}\) such that
\[
\beta(v_{l,b,k})=v_{l,b\oplus\epsilon_l,k}
\quad\text{for all }b,k.
\]
Now \(T(\lambda_l,u_0,v_{l,0,0})\) holds.  Applying \(\beta\) gives
\[
T(\lambda_l,u_d,v_{l,\epsilon_l,0}),
\]
so \(\epsilon_l=\chi_{F_d}(l)=\chi_F(l)\).  Therefore \(\beta\) agrees with \(\alpha_F\) on \(L\), \(U\), and \(V\).  Uniqueness follows because \(F\) is recovered from \(\beta(u_0)=u_{c(F)}\).
\end{proof}

Consequently,
\[
\Aut(\mathcal A)=\{\alpha_F:F\subseteq\omega\text{ finite}\}.
\]
In particular, the automorphism group is countable, and in the displayed computable copy each automorphism is computable, with its finite support hardwired.

\section{Automorphisms of arbitrary copies are computable from the copy}\label{sec:copies}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:copies}
Let \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\) be any copy with domain \(\omega\).  If \(\rho\in\Aut(\mathcal B)\), then \(\rho\leq_T D(\mathcal B)\).  Hence \(\mathcal A\) is computably \(\operatorname{AUT}\)-countable on a cone with cone base \(\emptyset\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Choose an isomorphism \(h:\mathcal A\to\mathcal B\).  Then \(h^{-1}\rho h\in\Aut(\mathcal A)\), so by Lemma~\ref{lem:all-aut} there is a finite \(F\subseteq\omega\) such that
\[
h^{-1}\rho h=\alpha_F.
\]
Let
\[
M=\{h(\lambda_l):l\in F\}.
\]
For each \(f=h(\lambda_l)\in M\), fix the two roots
\[
r_f^0=h(v_{l,0,0}),\qquad r_f^1=h(v_{l,1,0}).
\]
The finite list \(M\) and these finitely many roots are hardwired into the oracle program.  This is legitimate because \(\rho\leq_TD(\mathcal B)\) requires only that some oracle program compute \(\rho\) from \(D(\mathcal B)\); the program index may depend on the particular pair \((\mathcal B,\rho)\).

Given input \(x\), the program queries \(D(\mathcal B)\) to determine which of \(L^{\mathcal B}(x)\), \(U^{\mathcal B}(x)\), and \(V^{\mathcal B}(x)\) holds.  Exactly one holds because \(\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A\).

If \(x\in L^{\mathcal B}\), output \(x\).  This is correct because every automorphism of \(\mathcal A\), hence every conjugate automorphism of \(\mathcal B\), fixes the label ray pointwise.

If \(x\in U^{\mathcal B}\), set \(y=x\).  For each \(f\in M\), search through \(z=0,1,2,\ldots\) until finding the unique \(z\) with
\[
\mathcal B\models Q(f,y,z),
\]
and replace \(y\) by this \(z\).  The search halts because in \(\mathcal A\), and therefore in \(\mathcal B\), for each label \(f\) and each \(U\)-element \(y\), there is exactly one such \(z\).  If \(x=h(u_e)\), then the successive searches toggle exactly the labels in \(F\), so the final output is
\[
h(u_{c(F_e\triangle F)})=\rho(x).
\]

If \(x\in V^{\mathcal B}\), first check the finitely many \(f\in M\).  If no \(f\in M\) satisfies \(I^{\mathcal B}(f,x)\), output \(x\).  This is correct because \(x\) lies over a label not in the support \(M\).  Otherwise there is a unique \(f=h(\lambda_l)\in M\) with \(I^{\mathcal B}(f,x)\).  Starting with
\[
y_0=r_f^0,\qquad y_1=r_f^1,
\]
repeat the following procedure.  If \(x=y_0\), output \(y_1\).  If \(x=y_1\), output \(y_0\).  If neither equality holds, search for the unique \(y_0'\) and \(y_1'\) such that
\[
\mathcal B\models S(f,y_0,y_0')
\quad\text{and}\quad
\mathcal B\models S(f,y_1,y_1'),
\]
then replace \(y_0,y_1\) by \(y_0',y_1'\).  The searches halt because each element on either ray has a unique successor.  After \(n\) repetitions, \(y_i=h(v_{l,i,n})\).  Since every element of the gadget over \(f\) is at some finite level on one of the two rays, the procedure eventually finds \(x\) and returns the element at the same level on the other ray, which is exactly \(\rho(x)\).

Thus a \(D(\mathcal B)\)-oracle program computes \(\rho\), so \(\rho\leq_TD(\mathcal B)\).  Since \(\mathcal B\) and \(\rho\) were arbitrary, the cone base is \(\emptyset\).
\end{proof}

\section{The nondefinability argument}\label{sec:nondef}

For \(l\in\omega\), define an injective map \(\eta_l:\omega\to\omega\) by
\[
\eta_l(v_{l,0,k})=v_{l,0,k+1}
\quad\text{for all }k,
\]
and
\[
\eta_l(x)=x
\quad\text{for all other }x.
\]
Thus \(\eta_l\) shifts the \(0\)-side ray over \(l\) forward one step and fixes everything else.

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:eta-atomic}
For every \(l\in\omega\), every atomic formula \(\theta(\bar x)\), allowing equality, and every tuple \(\bar c\) from \(\mathcal A\),
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(\bar c)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal A\models \theta(\eta_l(\bar c)).
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Equality is preserved and reflected because \(\eta_l\) is injective.  The predicates \(L,U,V\) are preserved and reflected because \(\eta_l\) maps each sort into itself and no element changes sort.  The relation \(N\) is preserved and reflected because all labels are fixed.  The relation \(Q\) is preserved and reflected because all labels and all \(U\)-elements are fixed.  The relation \(I\) is preserved and reflected because a \(V\)-element keeps its label coordinate under \(\eta_l\).

For \(T\), the truth of \(T(\lambda_m,u_e,v_{m,b,k})\) depends only on \(m\), \(e\), and \(b\), not on \(k\).  The map \(\eta_l\) fixes labels and \(U\)-elements and, on \(V\), preserves both the label coordinate and the side \(b\).  Hence \(T\) is preserved and reflected.

It remains to check \(S\).  If an \(S\)-edge is not on \(R_{l,0}\), \(\eta_l\) fixes both its source and its target.  On \(R_{l,0}\),
\[
S(\lambda_l,v_{l,0,k},v_{l,0,j})
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
j=k+1
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
j+1=(k+1)+1,
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
S(\lambda_l,\eta_l(v_{l,0,k}),\eta_l(v_{l,0,j})).
\]
There are no \(S\)-edges between distinct rays or over the wrong label.  Thus \(S\) is also preserved and reflected.  All atomic formulas are built from equality or one basic relation, so the lemma follows.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:eta-qf}
Fix \(l\in\omega\), and let \(\bar p\) be a tuple fixed pointwise by \(\eta_l\).  If \(\theta(\bar x,\bar p)\) is any quantifier-free \(L_{\omega_1\omega}\)-formula with parameters from \(\bar p\), then for every tuple \(\bar c\),
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(\bar c,\bar p)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal A\models \theta(\eta_l(\bar c),\bar p).
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
By Lemma~\ref{lem:eta-atomic}, the equivalence holds for atomic formulas without parameters.  Since every entry of \(\bar p\) is fixed by \(\eta_l\), it also holds for atomic formulas in which entries of \(\bar p\) occur as parameters.  The general quantifier-free case follows by induction on the well-founded formation tree of the \(L_{\omega_1\omega}\)-formula: negation preserves equivalence, and countable conjunctions and countable disjunctions preserve equivalence because they are true exactly when each, respectively some, member of the corresponding family is true.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:existential-transfer}
Fix \(l\in\omega\), and let \(\bar p\) be a tuple fixed pointwise by \(\eta_l\).  Put
\[
r_0=v_{l,0,0},\qquad r_1=v_{l,1,0},\qquad s_0=v_{l,0,1}.
\]
Let \(\varphi(x,y)\) be an existential formula with parameters from \(\bar p\), whose quantifier-free matrix may be an \(L_{\omega_1\omega}\)-formula.  If
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi(r_0,r_1),
\]
then
\[
\mathcal A\models \varphi(s_0,r_1).
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Write
\[
\varphi(x,y)\equiv \exists \bar z\,\theta(x,y,\bar z,\bar p),
\]
where \(\bar z\) is finite and \(\theta\) is quantifier-free.  Suppose \(\mathcal A\models\varphi(r_0,r_1)\).  Choose witnesses \(\bar c\) such that
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(r_0,r_1,\bar c,\bar p).
\]
By Lemma~\ref{lem:eta-qf},
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(\eta_l(r_0),\eta_l(r_1),\eta_l(\bar c),\bar p).
\]
Now \(\eta_l(r_0)=s_0\) and \(\eta_l(r_1)=r_1\), so
\[
\mathcal A\models \theta(s_0,r_1,\eta_l(\bar c),\bar p).
\]
Thus \(\mathcal A\models\varphi(s_0,r_1)\).
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:nondef}
For every finite tuple \(\bar a\) from \(\mathcal A\), there is an automorphism \(\pi\in\Aut(\mathcal A)\) such that \(\pi\) is not \(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\)-definable over parameters from
\[
\rng(\bar a)\cup \pi[\rng(\bar a)].
\]
This remains true even if the existential formulas are allowed to have quantifier-free \(L_{\omega_1\omega}\)-matrices.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Only finitely many entries of \(\bar a\) lie in the \(V\)-sort, and each such entry lies in exactly one \(P_m\).  Choose \(l\) such that no entry of \(\bar a\) lies in \(P_l\).  Let
\[
\pi=\alpha_{\{l\}}.
\]
Then \(\pi\) swaps the two rays over \(l\), toggles the \(l\)-th bit on the \(U\)-sort, and fixes every \(P_m\) with \(m\neq l\).  Put
\[
P=\rng(\bar a)\cup \pi[\rng(\bar a)].
\]
No element of \(P\) lies in \(P_l\): labels and \(U\)-elements remain labels and \(U\)-elements under \(\pi\), and any \(V\)-entry of \(\bar a\) lies in some \(P_m\) with \(m\neq l\), which \(\pi\) fixes pointwise.  Therefore every element of \(P\) is fixed by \(\eta_l\).

Let
\[
r_0=v_{l,0,0},\qquad r_1=v_{l,1,0},\qquad s_0=v_{l,0,1}.
\]
Then
\[
\pi(r_0)=r_1,
\qquad
\pi(s_0)=v_{l,1,1}\neq r_1.
\]

Assume, for contradiction, that the graph of \(\pi\) is \(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\)-definable over parameters from \(P\).  Then there is a countable family \((\varphi_i(x,y))_{i\in\omega}\) of existential formulas with parameters from \(P\) such that for all \(x,y\in A\),
\[
\pi(x)=y
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal A\models\varphi_i(x,y)\text{ for some }i\in\omega.
\]
Since \(\pi(r_0)=r_1\), there is \(i\) with
\[
\mathcal A\models\varphi_i(r_0,r_1).
\]
All parameters occurring in \(\varphi_i\) belong to \(P\), hence are fixed by \(\eta_l\).  Lemma~\ref{lem:existential-transfer} gives
\[
\mathcal A\models\varphi_i(s_0,r_1).
\]
The alleged definition therefore implies \(\pi(s_0)=r_1\), contradicting \(\pi(s_0)=v_{l,1,1}\neq r_1\).  Hence no such \(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\)-definition exists.
\end{proof}

\section*{Conclusion}

The structure \(\mathcal A\) is computable and uses only the finite relational language \(\{L,U,V,N,I,Q,S,T\}\).  Every automorphism of every copy of \(\mathcal A\) is computable from that copy's atomic diagram, so the cone base may be taken to be \(\emptyset\).  Nevertheless, for every finite tuple of parameters \(\bar a\), some automorphism of \(\mathcal A\) is not \(\Sigma^{\mathrm{in}}_1\)-definable from \(\rng(\bar a)\cup\pi[\rng(\bar a)]\).  Thus the original question has a positive answer.

\begin{theorem}[Headline theorem: original problem]\label{thm:headline}
\normalfont
Let \(\Z\) denote the integers and let \(\R\) denote the reals.  Let
\[
\Sigma=\R\times[0,1],\qquad \partial\Sigma=\R\times\{0,1\}.
\]
For \(\beta>0\), define \(G_\beta:\Sigma\to\Sigma\) by
\[
G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).
\]
Let \(G_\beta^k\) denote the \(k\)-fold composition of \(G_\beta\) or
\(G_\beta^{-1}\), according as \(k>0\) or \(k<0\), and let
\(G_\beta^0\) be the identity.  A \emph{clean triangle} is the convex hull of
three non-collinear points in \(\partial\Sigma\).  A \emph{clean triangulation}
of \(\Sigma\) is a countable, locally finite collection of clean triangles
with pairwise disjoint interiors and union \(\Sigma\).  It is
\(G_\beta\)-\emph{invariant} if \(G_\beta\) preserves the triangulation.  A
\emph{squeeze map} of a clean triangle is an affine map into \(\R^3\) which
decreases the length of the edge contained in \(\partial\Sigma\) and increases
the lengths of the other two edges.  A number \(\beta>0\) is \emph{realized}
if there is a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\R^3\) such that
\begin{enumerate}
\item \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
\(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\);
\item \(f(p)=f(q)\) if and only if \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\Z\).
\end{enumerate}
Then the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Established stronger claim]\label{thm:strong}
The realized numbers are exactly the interval \((\sqrt3,\infty)\).
\end{theorem}

\section*{Overview / Strategy}

The lower bound passes to the quotient
\(M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle\), a flat M\"obius band with boundary
length \(2\beta\).  The map descends to an embedded piecewise-affine surface.
Inside each clean triangle the cevians from the apex to the boundary edge are
strictly lengthened.  These cevians form a leaf graph; in the quotient the leaf
graph is a circle, while upstairs it is a line, and the deck transformation
reverses the orientation of the image support line.  A topological lemma about
paths of oriented lines then forces two image support lines to be perpendicular
and intersecting.  Boundary shortening and a Euclidean estimate give
\(\beta>\sqrt3\).

For the upper bound we use one classical external input: the
Sadowsky--Wunderlich construction of embedded developable M\"obius bands, in a
quantitative form.  We approximate such a smooth ruled band by an invariant
piecewise-linear ruled band whose boundary edges are not expanded and whose
transverse edges are shortened by an arbitrarily small amount.  A final
anisotropic rescaling makes the boundary edges strictly shorter and all
transverse edges strictly longer, yielding a realization for every
\(\beta>\sqrt3\).

\section{The lower bound}

Throughout this section assume that \(\beta\) is realized by \(f\) on a
\(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\).  Write
\(G=G_\beta\).

\begin{lemma}[Face-to-face structure]\label{lem:facetoface}
The triangulation is face-to-face: distinct edges have disjoint relative
interiors, every transverse edge is incident to exactly two triangles, and
every boundary edge is incident to exactly one triangle.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
A clean triangle has exactly one boundary edge: among its three boundary
vertices, exactly two lie on one boundary component and the third lies on the
other.  The remaining two edges are transverse.

Two transverse edges cannot cross in the interior of \(\Sigma\), since near a
transverse crossing each of the two incident triangles occupies one open side
of its edge, and two such open half-disks always overlap in an open sector.  If
two transverse edges are collinear and overlap in more than one point, then
they lie on the same non-horizontal line; such a line meets \(y=0\) and
\(y=1\) once each, so the two edges are the same geometric segment.  Boundary
edges cannot overlap in a nontrivial interval, because at an interior point of
the overlap both adjacent triangles contain an inward half-neighborhood of the
boundary.  Finally, the endpoint of a transverse edge cannot lie in the
relative interior of a boundary edge: the triangle with the boundary edge
contains a small inward half-disk there, while the triangle with the transverse
edge contains a nonempty sector inside that half-disk.  Hence distinct
triangles meet only in common faces.

Let \(e\) be a transverse edge and \(z\) an interior point of \(e\).  A small
disk around \(z\) is divided by \(e\) into two half-disks.  At most one
triangle occupies each side, by disjointness of interiors, and at least one
triangle occupies each side, because the triangles cover \(\Sigma\).  Thus
\(e\) is incident to exactly two triangles.  The same half-disk argument at an
interior point of a boundary edge gives exactly one incident triangle.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[The finite quotient triangulation]\label{lem:quotient}
The quotient map \(f\) descends to an embedding
\(F:M_\beta\to\R^3\), where
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G\rangle
      =[0,\beta]\times[0,1]/(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y),
\]
and the quotient triangulation of \(M_\beta\) is finite and face-to-face.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Since \(p=Gq\) implies \(f(p)=f(q)\), the map descends to a continuous
\(F\).  If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so by realization
\(p=G^kq\), hence \([p]=[q]\).  Thus \(F\) is injective; because \(M_\beta\)
is compact and \(\R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is an embedding.

The restriction of \(f\) to each triangle is injective.  Indeed, if an affine
restriction had rank \(<2\), then arbitrarily close distinct points
\(p,q\) in that triangle would satisfy \(f(p)=f(q)\).  Then \(p=G^kq\) for
some \(k\ne0\), impossible for sufficiently close \(p,q\), since
\(G^{2n}\) translates by \(2n\beta\) and \(G^{2n+1}\) changes the
\(x\)-coordinate by \((2n+1)\beta\), so every nontrivial \(G^k\) moves every
point by distance at least \(\beta\).

Therefore no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself.  If
\(z\in\tau\cap G^k\tau\), write \(z=G^kw\) with \(w\in\tau\).  Then
\(f(z)=f(w)\), and injectivity on \(\tau\) gives \(z=w\), contradicting
freeness of the \(G\)-action unless \(k=0\).  A compact fundamental rectangle,
for instance \([0,2\beta]\times[0,1]\), meets only finitely many closed
triangles by local finiteness and compactness, and every orbit of triangles has
a representative meeting it.  Hence the quotient triangulation is finite.
Lemma \ref{lem:facetoface} descends to the quotient.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Cevian lengthening]\label{lem:cevian}
Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle whose
boundary edge is \([r_0,r_1]\), and let \(A:\Delta\to\R^3\) be a squeeze map.
For \(r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1\), every cevian \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened:
\[
|A(a)-A(r(s))|>|a-r(s)|,\qquad 0\le s\le1.
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Set \(q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2\).  The two transverse edges are
strictly lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\).  Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0
\]
because the boundary edge is strictly shortened.  Thus \(q\) is strictly
concave and lies above its chord:
\(q(s)\ge(1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0\).  Taking square roots gives the claim.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Paths of oriented lines]\label{lem:lines}
Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in
\(\R^3\).  Suppose \(L_1\) is the same unoriented line as \(L_0\), but with the
opposite orientation.  Then two distinct parameters give unoriented lines that
are perpendicular and intersect.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Represent an oriented line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit
direction and \(m=p\times u\) for any point \(p\) on the line.  Reversing
orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).  Choose continuous
\((u(s),m(s))\) and extend by \((u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s))\).  For
\(s\in\R/\Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),\qquad
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then \((a,b)(s,0)=(1,0)\), \((a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0)\), and
\((a,b)(s+\delta,1-\delta)=-(a,b)(s,\delta)\).

Assume \((a,b)\ne(0,0)\) everywhere.  Then
\(\Phi=(a,b)/\sqrt{a^2+b^2}:S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\) is continuous and is
constant on \(\delta=0\).  On \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\Phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\Phi(s,1/2).
\]
If \(\psi:S^1\to S^1\) has \(\psi(s+1/2)=-\psi(s)\), then, after lifting to an
angle \(\theta\), the integer
\(\bigl(\theta(s+1/2)-\theta(s)-\pi\bigr)/(2\pi)\) is constant, so the degree
of \(\psi\) is odd.  But \(\Phi|_{\delta=1/2}\) is homotopic through
\(S^1\times[0,1/2]\) to the constant map \(\Phi|_{\delta=0}\), so its degree is
zero, a contradiction.  Hence \((a,b)=(0,0)\) somewhere, with
\(0<\delta<1\), so the two parameters are distinct.

At such a zero, \(u\cdot v=0\) for the two directions \(u,v\).  If the lines
are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Thus, when \(u\perp v\), the condition \(b=0\) says the two nonparallel lines
are coplanar.  Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Perpendicular intersecting leaves]\label{lem:perpleaves}
In \(M_\beta\) there are two distinct leaves whose image support lines under
\(F\) are perpendicular and intersect.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Inside each triangle take the cevians from the apex to the boundary edge and
call them leaves.  In the finite quotient triangulation, form a graph
\(\Lambda_M\) by taking one closed interval for the leaves of each triangle and
gluing endpoints corresponding to common transverse edges.  By
Lemma \ref{lem:facetoface}, every vertex has valence \(2\).

The graph is connected.  If \(C\) is a component and \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) is
the union of the corresponding leaves, then \(U_C\) is open: this is immediate
inside triangles, along boundary edges, and across transverse edges because the
two incident leaf intervals are glued.  At a boundary vertex, a small half-disk
meets finitely many incident triangle sectors; consecutive sectors share an
edge from the vertex, and all non-boundary shared edges are transverse, so all
incident triangles lie in the same component.  Thus the sets \(U_C\) are
disjoint open subsets covering the connected surface \(M_\beta\); hence there
is only one.  A finite connected graph with all vertices of valence \(2\) is a
circle, so \(\Lambda_M\cong S^1\).

The same openness argument upstairs gives a connected leaf graph
\(\Lambda_\Sigma\).  The action of \(G\) on leaves is free.  A lifted leaf has
endpoints \((x,0)\) and \((x+t,1)\).  If \(G^{2n}\) fixes it, then
\(2n\beta=0\).  If \(G^{2n+1}\) fixes it, then the two endpoint equations give
simultaneously \(t=(2n+1)\beta\) and \(t=-(2n+1)\beta\), impossible.  Hence
\(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\) is a connected infinite covering of a circle,
so \(\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\R\).

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\).  By
Lemma \ref{lem:cevian}, its image is a nondegenerate segment, hence determines
a continuous oriented support line along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\).  If \(\ell\) is a
leaf, then \(G\ell\), reoriented from bottom to top, has the same image segment
with the opposite orientation, because \(G\) swaps boundary components and
\(f\circ G=f\).  Applying Lemma \ref{lem:lines} to the compact interval in
\(\Lambda_\Sigma\) from \(\ell\) to \(G\ell\) gives two distinct quotient
leaves with perpendicular intersecting support lines.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[No \(\beta\le\sqrt3\) is realized]\label{prop:lower}
If \(\beta\) is realized, then \(\beta>\sqrt3\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(J\) be a lifted leaf with endpoints \((x,0)\) and \((x+t,1)\).  Its flat
length is \(\sqrt{1+t^2}\).  We first show
\[
|t|<\beta. \tag{1}
\]
If \(t=\beta\) or \(t=-\beta\), the endpoints are identified by \(G\) or
\(G^{-1}\), so their images coincide, contradicting Lemma \ref{lem:cevian}.  If
\(t>\beta\), translate so \(J\) has endpoints \((0,0)\), \((t,1)\).  Then
\(GJ\) has endpoints \((\beta,1)\), \((t+\beta,0)\), and the two segments meet
at height \((t+\beta)/(2t)\in(0,1)\).  Thus some distinct \(p,q\in J\) satisfy
\(p=Gq\), so \(f(p)=f(q)\), contradicting injectivity on the nondegenerate
image segment.  The case \(t<-\beta\) is identical using \(G^{-1}\).

Parametrize the boundary circle of \(M_\beta\) modulo \(2\beta\) by assigning
coordinate \(u\) to \((u,0)\) and coordinate \(v-\beta\) to \((v,1)\).  Hence
the boundary arcs between the endpoints of a leaf of span \(t\) have lengths
\[
\beta-t,\qquad \beta+t, \tag{2}
\]
both positive by (1).  Also every nonconstant boundary arc is strictly
shortened by \(F\): the quotient boundary is subdivided into finitely many
boundary edges, and on each such edge \(F\) has constant length factor
\(\lambda_e<1\); summing over subsegments gives
\[
\ell_F(\gamma)<\ell_0(\gamma). \tag{3}
\]

Choose the two leaves \(T,B\) from Lemma \ref{lem:perpleaves}, and let their
support lines meet perpendicularly at \(P\).  Since distinct leaves have
disjoint relative interiors and \(F\) is embedded, \(P\) cannot lie in the
relative interiors of both image segments.  Rename so that \(F(B)\) lies in a
closed ray from \(P\).  Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\),
and put \(Q=F(q)\).  Then
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1,
\]
so in fact
\[
y>1. \tag{4}
\]

Lift \(T\) with endpoints \((x,0)\), \((x+t,1)\).  Replacing this lift by its
\(G\)-translate changes \(t\) to \(-t\), so it interchanges the two lengths in
(2).  Choose the lift so that the boundary arc \(D\) containing \(q\) has
length \(\beta+t\); let the other arc be \(H\), of length \(\beta-t\).  Let
\(A,C\) be the endpoints of \(F(T)\), and set
\[
L=|A-C|=|F(T)|>\sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{5}
\]
The arc \(H\) connects \(A\) to \(C\), so by (3),
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H)\ge L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{6}
\]
The arc \(D\) connects \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\).  Therefore
\(\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|\).  Among points at distance \(y\) from the line
\(AC\), the sum of distances to \(A\) and \(C\) is minimized when the
projection is the midpoint of \(AC\), giving
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Using (4) and (5),
\[
\ell_F(D)>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
By (3),
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D)>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{7}
\]
Combining (6) and (7),
\[
\beta>
\max\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\}.
\]
For every real \(t\), this maximum is at least \(\sqrt3\).  Indeed, if both
terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then squaring
\(\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3\) gives \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while squaring
\(\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3\) gives \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction.  Hence
\(\beta>\sqrt3\).
\end{proof}

\section{The upper bound}

We quote the classical Sadowsky--Wunderlich construction in the precise form
needed.

\begin{theorem}[Sadowsky--Wunderlich input]\label{thm:sadowsky}
For every \(\mu>\sqrt3\), there is a map
\(E_\mu:\R\times[0,1]\to\R^3\) such that:
\begin{enumerate}
\item \(E_\mu(X+\mu,1-Y)=E_\mu(X,Y)\);
\item the induced quotient M\"obius band is embedded;
\item the quotient surface is \(C^{1,1}\), piecewise \(C^\infty\), and
developable;
\item it is foliated by full-width straight rulings, and \(E_\mu\) maps each
ruling affinely and isometrically to a segment;
\item the boundary lifts are parametrized by arclength in the \(X\)-coordinate;
\item the construction remains embedded on a slightly wider strip.
\end{enumerate}
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
This is the three-cylinder construction of Sadowsky, with Wunderlich's
analysis of the developable M\"obius band; see \cite{Sad30,Wun62}.  In that
construction the band consists of three planar pieces and three cylindrical
pieces joined along generators with matching tangent planes, and the bending is
isometric.  The standard separation computation gives maximum half-width
\[
b_{\max}=\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}
\]
for auxiliary cylinder diameter \(d>0\).  Since \(\mu>\sqrt3\), choosing
\(0<d<2(\mu-\sqrt3)/(3\pi\sqrt3)\) gives \(b_{\max}>1/2\), hence a width-one
embedded strip, and the strict inequality gives the slightly wider embedded
extension.
\end{proof}

For \(L,c>0\), write
\[
H_{L,c}(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y)\qquad\text{on }\R\times[0,c].
\]

\begin{lemma}[Invariant PL approximation]\label{lem:PL}
Let \(E:\R\times[0,c]\to\R^3\) be a scaled Sadowsky band invariant under
\(H_{L,c}\), with full-width rulings
\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)].
\]
Fix \(x_0\), put \(\phi(x)=h(x)+L\), and set
\[
S=\max_{x\in[x_0,\phi(x_0)]}|h(x)-x|.
\]
For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there are an
\(H_{L,c}\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) and an
\(H_{L,c}\)-invariant PL map \(F_0\) such that:
\begin{enumerate}
\item \(F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\) iff \(P=H_{L,c}^k(Q)\) for some \(k\in\Z\);
\item every boundary edge is nonexpanded;
\item every transverse edge \(PQ\) satisfies
\[
|F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|-\varepsilon;
\]
\item every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).
\end{enumerate}
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The noncrossing full-width rulings imply that \(h\) is a strictly increasing
homeomorphism.  The map \(H=H_{L,c}\) sends \(R_x\) to
\(R_{\phi(x)}\), so
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L,\qquad \phi^2(x)=x+2L. \tag{8}
\]
Also
\[
|h(x)-x|<L. \tag{9}
\]
If \(h(x)-x=L\), the endpoints of \(R_x\) are identified by \(H\), contradicting
the isometric nondegenerate image of \(R_x\).  If \(h(x)-x>L\), then \(R_x\)
meets \(H(R_x)\) in the interior of the strip; hence two distinct points of
\(R_x\) have the same image under \(E\), again impossible.  The case
\(h(x)-x<-L\) follows using \(H^{-1}\).  Thus \(\phi(x)>x\).

Choose \(\rho>0\) with \(S+\rho<D\) and \(2\rho<\varepsilon\).  Choose a
partition
\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]
so fine that \(x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho\), \(h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho\), all seams of the
piecewise smooth Sadowsky band occur among the rulings \(R_{x_i}\), and the
embeddedness condition proved below holds.  Extend by \(x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i)\).
Then \(x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L\).  Let
\[
B_i=(x_i,0),\qquad T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]
The quadrilaterals \(B_iB_{i+1}T_{i+1}T_i\) tile the strip.  For
\(0\le i<N\), triangulate by the diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\), and extend
\(H\)-invariantly.  Define \(F_0\) on vertices by \(F_0(B_i)=E(B_i)\),
\(F_0(T_i)=E(T_i)\), and extend affinely on triangles.  Then \(F_0\) is
\(H\)-invariant.

Boundary edges are nonexpanded because their images are chords of boundary
arcs parametrized by arclength.  A ruling edge is mapped isometrically.  For a
diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\), set
\(d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|\) and \(r=|B_i-T_i|\).  Since
\(|T_i-T_{i+1}|<\rho\), one has \(|d-r|<\rho\), and
\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|
>r-\rho>d-2\rho>d-\varepsilon.
\]
The other diagonal type is identical, using \(x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho\).  By (8),
the absolute span \(|h(x)-x|\) is preserved under \(\phi\), so all ruling spans
are at most \(S\); diagonal spans are \(<S+\rho<D\).

It remains to justify the promised embeddedness for sufficiently fine
partition.  Let
\(N=(\R\times[0,c])/\langle H\rangle\).  By Theorem \ref{thm:sadowsky}, \(E\)
extends to a slightly wider compact embedded \(C^{1,1}\) surface
\(\widehat N\), so there is a tubular neighborhood \(U\) with nearest-point
projection \(\pi:U\to \widehat E(\widehat N)\).  The secant PL maps converge
uniformly to \(E\); because \(E\) is affine on rulings, \(C^{1,1}\) on each
piece, and the seams are included among the ruling edges with matching tangent
planes, the maps
\[
\Psi=\widehat E^{-1}\circ\pi\circ F_0:N\to\widehat N
\]
converge to the inclusion \(N\hookrightarrow\widehat N\) in \(C^1\) on each
piece.

For sufficiently small \(C^1\)-error, \(\Psi\) is injective.  Choose finitely
many coordinate disks with convex coordinate images and \(\delta>0\) so that
points of \(N\) at distance \(<\delta\) lie in a common disk.  In such a disk,
if the derivative of \(\Psi\) differs from the identity by \(<1/2\), then
integration along the coordinate segment gives
\(|\Psi(p)-\Psi(q)|\ge \frac12|p-q|\).  For pairs with distance \(\ge\delta\),
compactness and injectivity of the inclusion give a positive minimum
separation; sufficiently small \(C^0\)-error preserves this separation.  Hence
\(\Psi\) is injective.  If the quotient map induced by \(F_0\) identified two
points, then \(\Psi\) would identify them, contradiction.  Thus the quotient
PL map is embedded, which is equivalent to assertion (1) after lifting.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[Every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized]\label{prop:upper}
For every \(\beta>\sqrt3\), there is a realization of \(\beta\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Choose \(\mu\) with \(\sqrt3<\mu<\beta\), and take the Sadowsky band
\(E_\mu\).  Let \(S_\mu=\max |h(x)-x|\) over one fundamental interval of
rulings, and choose \(D_\mu>S_\mu\).  Choose \(a<1\), close to \(1\), with
\(a>\mu/\beta\), and set
\[
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu},\qquad L=a\beta.
\]
Then \(c>1\) and \(L=c\mu\).  Scale \(E_\mu\) by \(c\), giving a width-\(c\),
length-\(L\) band invariant under \(H_{L,c}\).  Its span bound scales to
\(cS_\mu\).

Choose \(a\) close enough to \(1\) so that
\[
m:=\min_{0\le u\le D_\mu}
\left(c\sqrt{1+u^2}-\sqrt{\left(\frac{cu}{a}\right)^2+1}\right)>0. \tag{10}
\]
This is possible because at \(a=1\) one has \(c=\beta/\mu>1\), and
\(c\sqrt{1+u^2}>\sqrt{c^2u^2+1}\) on the compact interval
\([0,D_\mu]\).  Choose \(0<\varepsilon<m\) and apply Lemma \ref{lem:PL} to the
scaled band with \(D=cD_\mu\).  We get \(\mathcal T_0\) and \(F_0\).

Define
\[
A:\Sigma\to\R\times[0,c],\qquad A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]
Because \(L=a\beta\),
\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))=(ax+a\beta,c-cy)=H_{L,c}(A(x,y)).
\]
Thus \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\).  Put
\[
f=F_0\circ A,\qquad \mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0).
\]
Then \(\mathcal T\) is a countable locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean
triangulation, and \(f\) is affine on every triangle.

If \(pq\) is a boundary edge, then
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|\le |A p-A q|=a|p-q|<|p-q|.
\]
If \(pq\) is a transverse edge, let \(s\) be the horizontal span of
\(A p\,A q\).  By Lemma \ref{lem:PL}, \(s<cD_\mu\); write \(s=cu\) with
\(0\le u<D_\mu\).  Then
\[
|Ap-Aq|=c\sqrt{1+u^2},\qquad
|p-q|=\sqrt{\left(\frac{cu}{a}\right)^2+1}.
\]
Using Lemma \ref{lem:PL} and \(\varepsilon<m\), (10) gives
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\ge |Ap-Aq|-\varepsilon
> |p-q|.
\]
Thus \(f\) is a squeeze map on every triangle.  Finally, for all
\(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\Longleftrightarrow
F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq)
\Longleftrightarrow
Ap=H_{L,c}^k(Aq)\text{ for some }k\in\Z
\Longleftrightarrow
p=G_\beta^k(q)\text{ for some }k\in\Z.
\]
Therefore \(\beta\) is realized.
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}[Proof of Theorem \ref{thm:strong}]
Proposition \ref{prop:lower} shows that every realized number is \(>\sqrt3\).
Proposition \ref{prop:upper} shows that every number \(>\sqrt3\) is realized.
Hence the realized numbers are exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\).
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}[Proof of Theorem \ref{thm:headline}]
By Theorem \ref{thm:strong}, the set of realized numbers is
\((\sqrt3,\infty)\), whose infimum is \(\sqrt3\).
\end{proof}

\title{For which probabilities \(p\) is it true that
\(\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m} w_i v_i \ge p\right]\ge p\)?}
\author{}
\date{}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\section*{Overview / Strategy}

The problem is to determine for which probabilities \(p\) the displayed inequality holds uniformly over all finite convex weight vectors. The main positive argument has two reductions. First, Hoeffding's extremal theorem for convex combinations of i.i.d. random variables reduces the weighted problem to the special case of equal weights. In the Bernoulli case, equal weights give binomial averages. Second, a classical binomial mean-tail estimate of Jogdeo--Samuels type says that for \(0\le p\le 1/3\), every binomial average is at least its mean with probability at least \(p\). These two facts are stated precisely in Section~1 and used in Proposition~\ref{prop:smallp}.

The remaining cases are elementary. At \(p=1/2\), the Bernoulli subset is uniformly distributed over all subsets, and pairing every subset with its complement gives probability at least \(1/2\). At \(p=1\), the assertion is immediate. To show that there are no other probabilities, equal weights on three variables give counterexamples for every \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\), and equal weights on two variables give counterexamples for every \(p\in(1/2,1)\).

\begin{definition}
A Bernoulli\((p)\) random variable is a random variable taking value \(1\) with probability \(p\) and value \(0\) with probability \(1-p\). For \(p\in[0,1]\), let \(\mathcal P(p)\) denote the following assertion:
for every integer \(m\ge1\), every choice of nonnegative real numbers
\[
w_1,\ldots,w_m\ge0,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i=1,
\]
and every family \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\) of i.i.d. Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables, one has
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{theorem}[Headline theorem: the original problem]\label{thm:main}
The assertion \(\mathcal P(p)\) holds exactly for
\[
p\in[0,\tfrac13]\cup\{\tfrac12,1\}.
\]
Equivalently, the complete set of probabilities \(p\) for which
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m} w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\]
holds for every finite convex weight vector \((w_1,\ldots,w_m)\) and every i.i.d. Bernoulli\((p)\) family \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\) is
\[
\boxed{[0,\tfrac13]\cup\{\tfrac12,1\}}.
\]
\end{theorem}

\section{Classical one-dimensional inputs}

The proof uses two standard one-dimensional results. They are quoted in the precise forms needed below. The first is Hoeffding's reduction theorem for convex combinations of i.i.d. random variables; the second is the binomial mean-tail estimate used to evaluate the equal-weight case.

\begin{lemma}[Hoeffding's reduction for convex combinations]\label{lem:hoeffding}
Let \(X_1,X_2,\ldots\) be i.i.d. real-valued random variables. For \(t\in\mathbb R\), define
\[
L(t):=\inf_{N\ge1}
\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}\ge t\right].
\]
Then for every integer \(m\ge1\) and every choice of weights
\[
w_1,\ldots,w_m\ge0,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i=1,
\]
one has
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m} w_iX_i\ge t\right]\ge L(t).
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
This is the one-dimensional half-line form of Hoeffding's extremal theorem for convex combinations of i.i.d. random variables; see Hoeffding \cite{Hoeffding1955}. To make explicit that the closed-tail convention used here causes no ambiguity, we record the standard passage from the open-tail formulation.

Hoeffding's theorem gives, for every real \(u\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_iX_i>u\right]
\ge
\inf_{N\ge1}
\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}>u\right].
\]
Fix \(t\in\mathbb R\). For every \(\varepsilon>0\), apply the preceding inequality with \(u=t-\varepsilon\). For each \(N\),
\[
\left\{\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}\ge t\right\}
\subseteq
\left\{\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}>t-\varepsilon\right\},
\]
and therefore
\[
\inf_{N\ge1}
\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}>t-\varepsilon\right]
\ge
\inf_{N\ge1}
\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}\ge t\right]
=L(t).
\]
Thus
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_iX_i>t-\varepsilon\right]\ge L(t)
\qquad\text{for every }\varepsilon>0.
\]
As \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\), the events
\[
\left\{\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_iX_i>t-\varepsilon\right\}
\]
decrease to
\[
\left\{\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_iX_i\ge t\right\}.
\]
By continuity of probability from above,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_iX_i\ge t\right]\ge L(t).
\]
This is the desired closed-tail statement.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Binomial mean-tail inequality]\label{lem:binomial}
Let
\[
X_{N,p}\sim\operatorname{Bin}(N,p).
\]
Then the following hold.

\begin{enumerate}
\item If \(0\le p\le \frac13\), then for every \(N\ge1\),
\[
\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge Np]\ge p.
\]
Equivalently, since \(X_{N,p}\) is integer-valued,
\[
\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge \lceil Np\rceil]\ge p.
\]

\item If \(p=\frac12\), then for every \(N\ge1\),
\[
\Pr[X_{N,1/2}\ge N/2]\ge \frac12.
\]
\end{enumerate}
Moreover, in the first assertion equality occurs for \(N=1\) and \(0<p\le1/3\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
For \(p=0\), \(X_{N,0}=0\) almost surely, so
\[
\Pr[X_{N,0}\ge0]=1\ge0.
\]
For \(0<p\le1/3\), the first assertion is the classical binomial mean-tail estimate of Samuels and Jogdeo--Samuels; see \cite{Samuels1965,JogdeoSamuels1968}. In the form needed here, that estimate states exactly that for every \(N\ge1\),
\[
\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge \lceil Np\rceil]\ge p.
\]
Because \(X_{N,p}\) is integer-valued, the events
\[
\{X_{N,p}\ge Np\}
\quad\text{and}\quad
\{X_{N,p}\ge \lceil Np\rceil\}
\]
are identical. This proves the first assertion.

For \(p=1/2\), the binomial distribution is symmetric:
\[
\Pr[X_{N,1/2}=j]=\Pr[X_{N,1/2}=N-j]
\qquad (0\le j\le N).
\]
If \(N=2r+1\) is odd, then the values \(0,\ldots,r\) pair with the values \(2r+1,\ldots,r+1\), and exactly one member of each pair is at least \(N/2=r+\frac12\). Hence
\[
\Pr[X_{N,1/2}\ge N/2]=\frac12.
\]
If \(N=2r\) is even, then the values \(0,\ldots,r-1\) pair with \(2r,\ldots,r+1\), while \(r\) is the central value and is included in the event \(\{X_{N,1/2}\ge r\}\). Therefore
\[
\Pr[X_{N,1/2}\ge N/2]
=
\frac12\bigl(1-\Pr[X_{N,1/2}=r]\bigr)
+\Pr[X_{N,1/2}=r]
=
\frac12+\frac12\Pr[X_{N,1/2}=r]
\ge\frac12.
\]
This proves the second assertion.

Finally, if \(N=1\) and \(0<p\le1/3\), then
\[
\{X_{1,p}\ge p\}=\{X_{1,p}=1\},
\]
so
\[
\Pr[X_{1,p}\ge p]=p.
\]
Thus equality is possible in the first assertion.
\end{proof}

\section{The positive cases}

\begin{proposition}[The interval \(0\le p\le1/3\)]\label{prop:smallp}
The assertion \(\mathcal P(p)\) holds for every
\[
p\in[0,\tfrac13].
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Fix \(p\in[0,1/3]\). Let \(m\ge1\), let
\[
w_1,\ldots,w_m\ge0,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i=1,
\]
and let \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\) be i.i.d. Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables.

Extend \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\), if necessary, to an infinite i.i.d. Bernoulli\((p)\) sequence \(X_1,X_2,\ldots\) with \(X_i=v_i\) for \(1\le i\le m\). Applying Lemma~\ref{lem:hoeffding} with \(t=p\) gives
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i v_i\ge p\right]
=
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i X_i\ge p\right]
\ge
\inf_{N\ge1}
\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}\ge p\right].
\]
For each \(N\), the sum \(X_1+\cdots+X_N\) has binomial distribution \(\operatorname{Bin}(N,p)\). Hence, writing \(X_{N,p}\sim\operatorname{Bin}(N,p)\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\frac{X_1+\cdots+X_N}{N}\ge p\right]
=
\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge Np].
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i v_i\ge p\right]
\ge
\inf_{N\ge1}\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge Np].
\]
By Lemma~\ref{lem:binomial}, since \(0\le p\le1/3\),
\[
\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge Np]\ge p
\qquad\text{for every }N\ge1.
\]
Taking the infimum over \(N\) gives
\[
\inf_{N\ge1}\Pr[X_{N,p}\ge Np]\ge p.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]
Since \(m\), the weights, and the Bernoulli\((p)\) family were arbitrary, \(\mathcal P(p)\) holds.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[The point \(p=1/2\)]\label{prop:halfp}
The assertion \(\mathcal P(1/2)\) holds.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(m\ge1\), let
\[
w_1,\ldots,w_m\ge0,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i=1,
\]
and let \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\) be i.i.d. Bernoulli\((1/2)\) random variables.

Let
\[
A=\{i\in\{1,\ldots,m\}: v_i=1\}.
\]
Because the \(v_i\) are independent and each is \(1\) with probability \(1/2\), the random set \(A\) is uniformly distributed over all \(2^m\) subsets of \(\{1,\ldots,m\}\). For a subset \(B\subseteq\{1,\ldots,m\}\), write
\[
w(B)=\sum_{i\in B}w_i.
\]
Then
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i v_i=w(A),
\]
so the desired event is
\[
\{w(A)\ge1/2\}.
\]

Pair every subset \(B\subseteq\{1,\ldots,m\}\) with its complement \(B^c\). These pairs partition the \(2^m\) subsets into \(2^{m-1}\) unordered pairs. Since
\[
w(B)+w(B^c)=\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i=1,
\]
at least one of \(B\) and \(B^c\) has weight at least \(1/2\). Thus at least one subset in each complementary pair belongs to the event \(\{w(A)\ge1/2\}\).

Every subset has probability \(2^{-m}\). Since at least \(2^{m-1}\) subsets satisfy \(w(B)\ge1/2\), we obtain
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i v_i\ge\frac12\right]
=
\Pr[w(A)\ge1/2]
\ge
2^{m-1}\cdot 2^{-m}
=
\frac12.
\]
Therefore \(\mathcal P(1/2)\) holds.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[The point \(p=1\)]\label{prop:onep}
The assertion \(\mathcal P(1)\) holds.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(m\ge1\), let
\[
w_1,\ldots,w_m\ge0,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i=1,
\]
and let \(v_1,\ldots,v_m\) be i.i.d. Bernoulli\((1)\) random variables. A Bernoulli\((1)\) random variable equals \(1\) almost surely, so
\[
v_i=1\qquad\text{almost surely for every }i.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i v_i
=
\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i
=
1
\]
almost surely. Hence
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m}w_i v_i\ge1\right]=1\ge1.
\]
Thus \(\mathcal P(1)\) holds.
\end{proof}

\section{Counterexamples outside the claimed set}

\begin{proposition}[Failure for \(1/3<p<1/2\)]\label{prop:countermiddle}
For every
\[
p\in\left(\frac13,\frac12\right),
\]
the assertion \(\mathcal P(p)\) fails.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Fix \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\). Take
\[
m=3,\qquad w_1=w_2=w_3=\frac13,
\]
and let \(v_1,v_2,v_3\) be i.i.d. Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables. Put
\[
N=v_1+v_2+v_3.
\]
Then \(N\sim\operatorname{Bin}(3,p)\) and
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{3}w_i v_i=\frac{N}{3}.
\]

Because \(p>1/3\), one success is not enough:
\[
\frac13<p.
\]
Because \(p<1/2<2/3\), two successes are enough:
\[
\frac23\ge p.
\]
Thus
\[
\left\{\sum_{i=1}^{3}w_i v_i\ge p\right\}
=
\{N\ge2\}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{3}w_i v_i\ge p\right]
=
\Pr[N\ge2].
\]
Since \(N\sim\operatorname{Bin}(3,p)\),
\[
\Pr[N\ge2]
=
\binom{3}{2}p^2(1-p)+p^3
=
3p^2(1-p)+p^3
=
p^2(3-2p).
\]
Now
\[
p-p^2(3-2p)
=
p(1-3p+2p^2)
=
p(1-p)(1-2p).
\]
For \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\), the factors \(p\), \(1-p\), and \(1-2p\) are all positive. Hence
\[
p-p^2(3-2p)>0,
\]
or equivalently,
\[
p^2(3-2p)<p.
\]
Thus this choice of weights gives
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{3}w_i v_i\ge p\right]<p.
\]
Therefore \(\mathcal P(p)\) fails.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[Failure for \(1/2<p<1\)]\label{prop:counterlarge}
For every
\[
p\in\left(\frac12,1\right),
\]
the assertion \(\mathcal P(p)\) fails.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Fix \(p\in(1/2,1)\). Take
\[
m=2,\qquad w_1=w_2=\frac12,
\]
and let \(v_1,v_2\) be i.i.d. Bernoulli\((p)\) random variables. Put
\[
N=v_1+v_2.
\]
Then
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{2}w_i v_i=\frac{N}{2}.
\]
Because \(p>1/2\), one success is not enough:
\[
\frac12<p.
\]
Since \(N\) can only take the values \(0,1,2\), the event
\[
\left\{\frac{N}{2}\ge p\right\}
\]
occurs only when \(N=2\), that is, when both \(v_1\) and \(v_2\) equal \(1\). Hence
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{2}w_i v_i\ge p\right]
=
\Pr[v_1=1,\ v_2=1].
\]
By independence,
\[
\Pr[v_1=1,\ v_2=1]=p^2.
\]
Since \(0<p<1\),
\[
p^2<p.
\]
Thus this choice of weights gives
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{2}w_i v_i\ge p\right]<p.
\]
Therefore \(\mathcal P(p)\) fails.
\end{proof}

\section{Proof of the classification}

\begin{proof}[Proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:main}]
Proposition~\ref{prop:smallp} proves that \(\mathcal P(p)\) holds for every
\[
p\in[0,\tfrac13].
\]
Proposition~\ref{prop:halfp} proves that \(\mathcal P(1/2)\) holds, and Proposition~\ref{prop:onep} proves that \(\mathcal P(1)\) holds. Hence
\[
\mathcal P(p)\quad\text{holds for every}\quad
p\in[0,\tfrac13]\cup\{\tfrac12,1\}.
\]

It remains to show that no other probability works. Since \(p\) is a probability, \(p\in[0,1]\). The complement of
\[
[0,\tfrac13]\cup\{\tfrac12,1\}
\]
inside \([0,1]\) is
\[
\left(\frac13,\frac12\right)\cup\left(\frac12,1\right).
\]
For every \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\), Proposition~\ref{prop:countermiddle} gives an explicit choice of weights for which the desired inequality fails. For every \(p\in(1/2,1)\), Proposition~\ref{prop:counterlarge} gives an explicit choice of weights for which the desired inequality fails.

Therefore \(\mathcal P(p)\) holds exactly for
\[
p\in[0,\tfrac13]\cup\{\tfrac12,1\}.
\]
This proves the theorem.
\end{proof}

## Theorem (fiber-filling and coarea reduction)

Let
\[
R=\prod_{i=1}^4[0,R_i],\qquad S=\prod_{i=1}^4[0,S_i],
\]
with \(0<R_1\le R_2\le R_3\le R_4\) and \(0<S_1\le S_2\le S_3\le S_4\). Orient \(S\) by
\[
dx_1\wedge dx_2\wedge dx_3\wedge dx_4.
\]
Write
\[
Q=[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2],\qquad B=[0,S_3]\times[0,S_4],
\]
so \(S=Q\times B\), and orient \(Q\) and \(B\) by \(dx_1\wedge dx_2\) and \(dx_3\wedge dx_4\), respectively. Let
\[
\pi:S\to B,\qquad \pi(x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4)=(x_3,x_4),
\]
and suppose
\[
f:(R,\partial R)\to(S,\partial S)
\]
is a Lipschitz finite piecewise smooth map of degree \(1\) with \(\operatorname{Dil}_2(f)\le 1\). Put
\[
F=\pi\circ f=(f_3,f_4),\qquad G=(f_1,f_2).
\]

For \(y\in B\), set
\[
P_y=Q\times\{y\}.
\]
Use the slicing convention normalized by
\[
\langle \llbracket Q\times B\rrbracket,\pi,y\rangle
=
\llbracket Q\times\{y\}\rrbracket .
\]

Then for a.e. \(y\in \operatorname{int}B\), the slice
\[
z_y=\langle \llbracket R\rrbracket,F,y\rangle
\]
is a relative integral \(2\)-cycle in \((R,\partial R)\), and
\[
f_\# z_y=\llbracket P_y\rrbracket .
\]
Consequently,
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(z_y)\ge \operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y)
=S_1S_2\,\operatorname{dist}(y,\partial B).
\]

Moreover,
\[
\int_B \operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy\le \operatorname{Vol}(R).
\]

Finally, let
\[
B_0=\left[\frac{S_3}{3},\frac{2S_3}{3}\right]\times
\left[\frac{S_4}{3},\frac{2S_4}{3}\right].
\]
At points where \(dF\) has rank \(2\), let
\[
\sigma_1(x)\ge \sigma_2(x)>0
\]
be the two nonzero singular values of \(dF_x\). Define the measure
\[
d\mu=\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\,dx
\]
on the rank-two set of \(dF\), and \(d\mu=0\) where \(\operatorname{rank}dF<2\). Then
\[
\mu(F^{-1}(B_0))\ge \frac19 S_1S_2S_3S_4
\]
and
\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\sigma_1^2\,d\mu.
\]

Thus, if in addition one has an estimate
\[
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\sigma_1^2\,d\mu
\ge
c_d\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}\,
\mu(F^{-1}(B_0))
\]
for some constant \(c_d>0\), then
\[
R_1R_2R_3R_4
\ge
\frac{c_d}{9}S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4.
\]

### Proof

Write \(\mathbf M(T)\) for the mass of an integral current \(T\), and write \(\llbracket X\rrbracket\) for the integral current associated to an oriented Lipschitz manifold or rectangle \(X\).

For a compact rectangle \(X\), if \(Z\in \mathbf I_m(X)\) satisfies \(\operatorname{spt}\partial Z\subset \partial X\), define its relative filling volume by
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_X(Z)
=
\inf\left\{
\mathbf M(Y):
Y\in \mathbf I_{m+1}(X),\ 
\operatorname{spt}(\partial Y-Z)\subset \partial X
\right\}.
\]

We use the standard slicing theorem for integral currents: if \(T\in \mathbf I_m\) and \(\Phi\) is Lipschitz into \(\mathbb R^k\), then for a.e. \(a\), the slice \(\langle T,\Phi,a\rangle\in \mathbf I_{m-k}\) exists and
\[
\partial\langle T,\Phi,a\rangle
=
\pm \langle \partial T,\Phi,a\rangle.
\]
We also use slicing naturality: for Lipschitz \(H\),
\[
H_\#\langle T,\Phi\circ H,a\rangle
=
\langle H_\#T,\Phi,a\rangle
\]
for a.e. \(a\). Finally, we use the rectifiable-current area inequality
\[
\mathbf M(H_\#T)
\le
\int J_k(dH|_{\operatorname{Tan}T})\,d\|T\|
\]
for \(T\in\mathbf I_k\).

First prove the fundamental current identity
\[
f_\#\llbracket R\rrbracket=\llbracket S\rrbracket .
\]
Let
\[
T=f_\#\llbracket R\rrbracket.
\]
Since \(f\) is Lipschitz, \(T\in\mathbf I_4(S)\), and
\[
\partial T=f_\#\partial\llbracket R\rrbracket
\]
is supported in \(f(\partial R)\subset \partial S\). Hence, in \(\operatorname{int}S\),
\[
\partial(T\llcorner \operatorname{int}S)=0.
\]
By the constancy theorem on the connected open set \(\operatorname{int}S\),
\[
T\llcorner \operatorname{int}S
=
m\,\llbracket \operatorname{int}S\rrbracket
\]
for some \(m\in\mathbb Z\). Therefore \(T-m\llbracket S\rrbracket\) is supported in \(\partial S\). Since \(\partial S\) has \(\mathcal H^4\)-measure zero, no nonzero integral \(4\)-current can be supported there, so
\[
T=m\llbracket S\rrbracket.
\]
Because \(f\) has degree \(1\), \(T\) represents the relative fundamental class \([S,\partial S]\), hence \(m=1\). Thus
\[
f_\#\llbracket R\rrbracket=\llbracket S\rrbracket .
\]

Now apply slicing to \(F=\pi\circ f\). For a.e. \(y\in\operatorname{int}B\),
\[
z_y=\langle \llbracket R\rrbracket,F,y\rangle
\]
is an integral \(2\)-current, and
\[
\partial z_y
=
\pm \langle \partial\llbracket R\rrbracket,F,y\rangle,
\]
so \(\partial z_y\) is supported in \(\partial R\). Hence \(z_y\) is a relative integral \(2\)-cycle in \((R,\partial R)\).

By slicing naturality,
\[
f_\# z_y
=
f_\#\langle \llbracket R\rrbracket,F,y\rangle
=
\langle f_\#\llbracket R\rrbracket,\pi,y\rangle
=
\langle \llbracket S\rrbracket,\pi,y\rangle .
\]
With the stated product orientation and slice convention,
\[
\langle \llbracket S\rrbracket,\pi,y\rangle
=
\llbracket Q\times\{y\}\rrbracket
=
\llbracket P_y\rrbracket.
\]
Thus
\[
f_\#z_y=\llbracket P_y\rrbracket.
\]

Next, \(\operatorname{Dil}_2(f)\le 1\) implies \(\operatorname{Dil}_3(f)\le 1\). Indeed, at a differentiability point of \(f\), let
\[
a_1\ge a_2\ge a_3\ge a_4\ge 0
\]
be the singular values of \(df\). The hypothesis gives \(a_1a_2\le 1\). Hence \(a_2\le 1\), so \(a_3\le 1\), and therefore
\[
a_1a_2a_3\le a_1a_2\le 1.
\]
Thus \(\|\Lambda^3df\|\le 1\) a.e.

Let \(Y\in \mathbf I_3(R)\) be a relative filling of \(z_y\), so
\[
\operatorname{spt}(\partial Y-z_y)\subset \partial R.
\]
Then
\[
\partial(f_\#Y)-f_\#z_y
=
f_\#(\partial Y-z_y)
\]
is supported in \(f(\partial R)\subset \partial S\). Since \(f_\#z_y=\llbracket P_y\rrbracket\), the current \(f_\#Y\) is a relative filling of \(P_y\) in \(S\). Also,
\[
\mathbf M(f_\#Y)\le \int \|\Lambda^3df\|\,d\|Y\|\le \mathbf M(Y).
\]
Taking the infimum over all such \(Y\) gives
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(z_y)\ge \operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y).
\]

It remains to compute \(\operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y)\). Let
\[
\delta(y)=\operatorname{dist}(y,\partial B).
\]
For the upper bound, choose a shortest segment \(\gamma\subset B\) from \(\partial B\) to \(y\), oriented toward \(y\). Then
\[
C=\llbracket Q\rrbracket\times \llbracket \gamma\rrbracket
\]
is a relative filling of \(P_y\) in \(S\), and
\[
\mathbf M(C)=S_1S_2\,\delta(y).
\]
Hence
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y)\le S_1S_2\,\delta(y).
\]

For the lower bound, define
\[
\delta(q)=\operatorname{dist}(q,\partial B)
=
\min\{q_3,S_3-q_3,q_4,S_4-q_4\}.
\]
For \(\eta>0\), set
\[
m_\eta(q)
=
-\eta\log\left(
e^{-q_3/\eta}
+
e^{-(S_3-q_3)/\eta}
+
e^{-q_4/\eta}
+
e^{-(S_4-q_4)/\eta}
\right).
\]
Then
\[
\delta(q)-\eta\log 4\le m_\eta(q)\le \delta(q),
\qquad
|\nabla m_\eta(q)|\le 1.
\]
Choose a smooth function \(\chi_\eta:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) such that
\[
\chi_\eta(t)=0\quad\text{for }t\le 0,\qquad
0\le \chi_\eta'(t)\le 1,\qquad
\chi_\eta(t)\ge t-\eta\quad\text{for }t\ge 0.
\]
Set
\[
\rho_\eta(q)=\chi_\eta(m_\eta(q)).
\]
Then \(\rho_\eta\) is smooth,
\[
\rho_\eta=0\quad\text{on }\partial B,\qquad |d\rho_\eta|\le 1,
\]
and for every fixed \(y\in\operatorname{int}B\),
\[
\rho_\eta(y)\to \delta(y)
\qquad\text{as }\eta\to 0.
\]

Define the smooth \(2\)-form
\[
\alpha_\eta=\rho_\eta(x_3,x_4)\,dx_1\wedge dx_2.
\]
It restricts to zero on \(\partial S\): on the \(x_1\)- and \(x_2\)-faces because \(dx_1\wedge dx_2\) vanishes there, and on the \(x_3\)- and \(x_4\)-faces because \(\rho_\eta=0\) on \(\partial B\).

Let \(Y\in\mathbf I_3(S)\) be any relative filling of \(P_y\), so
\[
\operatorname{spt}(\partial Y-\llbracket P_y\rrbracket)\subset \partial S.
\]
Since \(\alpha_\eta\) vanishes on \(\partial S\),
\[
\partial Y(\alpha_\eta)
=
\llbracket P_y\rrbracket(\alpha_\eta)
=
\rho_\eta(y)S_1S_2.
\]
By Stokes’ theorem for currents,
\[
Y(d\alpha_\eta)=\rho_\eta(y)S_1S_2.
\]
But
\[
d\alpha_\eta=d\rho_\eta\wedge dx_1\wedge dx_2,
\]
whose comass is at most \(|d\rho_\eta|\le 1\). Therefore
\[
\mathbf M(Y)\ge \rho_\eta(y)S_1S_2.
\]
Letting \(\eta\to 0\) gives
\[
\mathbf M(Y)\ge S_1S_2\,\delta(y).
\]
Taking the infimum over \(Y\),
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y)\ge S_1S_2\,\delta(y).
\]
Thus
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y)=S_1S_2\,\operatorname{dist}(y,\partial B).
\]

In particular, for \(y\in B_0\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(y,\partial B)\ge \frac{S_3}{3},
\]
because \(S_4\ge S_3\). Hence
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(z_y)\ge \frac13 S_1S_2S_3.
\]

Now apply the coarea formula to the Lipschitz map \(F:R\to B\). For every nonnegative Borel function \(g\),
\[
\int_B\int_{F^{-1}(y)} g\,d\mathcal H^2\,dy
=
\int_R g\,J_2F\,dx,
\]
where
\[
J_2F=\|\Lambda^2 dF\|.
\]
For a.e. \(y\), the mass of the slice \(z_y\) is \(\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\), so with \(g=1\),
\[
\int_B \operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
=
\int_R J_2F\,dx.
\]
Since \(F=\pi\circ f\) and \(\pi\) is an orthogonal projection,
\[
J_2F\le \|\Lambda^2df\|\le 1.
\]
Therefore
\[
\int_B \operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\le
\operatorname{Vol}(R).
\]

It remains to prove the two estimates involving \(\mu\). Fix a regular value \(y\in B_0\), and let \(x\in z_y\) be a point where \(f\) is differentiable and \(dF_x\) has rank \(2\). Then
\[
T_xz_y=\ker dF_x.
\]
Let
\[
N_x=(T_xz_y)^\perp.
\]
The restriction \(dF_x|_{N_x}:N_x\to\mathbb R^2\) has singular values \(\sigma_1(x)\ge \sigma_2(x)>0\).

Choose a unit vector \(n\in N_x\) such that
\[
|dF_x(n)|=\sigma_1(x).
\]
For any unit vector \(v\in T_xz_y\), one has \(dF_x(v)=0\). Since
\[
df_x=(dG_x,dF_x),
\]
we get
\[
df_x(v)=(dG_x(v),0),
\qquad
df_x(n)=(dG_x(n),dF_x(n)).
\]
In the orthogonal splitting \(\mathbb R^4=\mathbb R^2_Q\oplus\mathbb R^2_B\), the mixed \(Q\wedge B\) component of
\[
df_x(v)\wedge df_x(n)
\]
is
\[
dG_x(v)\wedge dF_x(n),
\]
whose norm is
\[
|dG_x(v)|\,|dF_x(n)|
=
|dG_x(v)|\,\sigma_1(x).
\]
This mixed component has norm at most the norm of the full wedge. Therefore, using \(\operatorname{Dil}_2(f)\le 1\),
\[
|dG_x(v)|\,\sigma_1(x)
\le
|df_x(v)\wedge df_x(n)|
\le 1.
\]
Hence
\[
|dG_x(v)|\le \sigma_1(x)^{-1}.
\]
Taking the supremum over unit \(v\in T_xz_y\),
\[
\|dG_x|_{T_xz_y}\|\le \sigma_1(x)^{-1}.
\]
Since \(T_xz_y\) is two-dimensional,
\[
J_2(G|_{T_xz_y})\le \sigma_1(x)^{-2}.
\]

Let \(p_Q:S\to Q\) be the projection onto the first two coordinates. Since \(G=p_Q\circ f\), for a.e. \(y\in B_0\),
\[
G_\#z_y
=
(p_Q)_\# f_\#z_y
=
(p_Q)_\#\llbracket P_y\rrbracket
=
\llbracket Q\rrbracket.
\]
Therefore
\[
S_1S_2
=
\mathbf M(\llbracket Q\rrbracket)
=
\mathbf M(G_\#z_y)
\le
\int_{z_y}J_2(G|_{Tz_y})\,dA
\le
\int_{z_y}\sigma_1^{-2}\,dA.
\]
Integrating over \(B_0\),
\[
\int_{B_0}\int_{z_y}\sigma_1^{-2}\,dA\,dy
\ge
S_1S_2\,|B_0|
=
\frac19 S_1S_2S_3S_4.
\]

Now define
\[
\widetilde g(x)
=
\sigma_1(x)^{-2}\,
\mathbf 1_{F^{-1}(B_0)\cap\{\operatorname{rank}dF=2\}}(x).
\]
For \(M>0\), set \(g_M=\min\{\widetilde g,M\}\). By the coarea formula,
\[
\int_{B_0}\int_{z_y} g_M\,dA\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}g_M\,J_2F\,dx.
\]
Letting \(M\to\infty\) and using monotone convergence,
\[
\int_{B_0}\int_{z_y}\sigma_1^{-2}\,dA\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)\cap\{\operatorname{rank}dF=2\}}
\sigma_1^{-2}J_2F\,dx.
\]
On the rank-two set,
\[
J_2F=\sigma_1\sigma_2.
\]
Thus
\[
\sigma_1^{-2}J_2F
=
\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}.
\]
Hence
\[
\int_{B_0}\int_{z_y}\sigma_1^{-2}\,dA\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\,dx
=
\mu(F^{-1}(B_0)).
\]
Combining this with the previous inequality gives
\[
\mu(F^{-1}(B_0))
\ge
\frac19 S_1S_2S_3S_4.
\]

Finally, applying the ordinary coarea formula over \(B_0\),
\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}J_2F\,dx.
\]
On the rank-two set,
\[
J_2F=\sigma_1\sigma_2
=
\sigma_1^2\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1},
\]
while both sides vanish on the rank \(<2\) set. Therefore
\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\sigma_1^2\,d\mu.
\]

Assume now that
\[
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\sigma_1^2\,d\mu
\ge
c_d\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}\,
\mu(F^{-1}(B_0)).
\]
Then
\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\ge
c_d\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}\,
\mu(F^{-1}(B_0))
\ge
\frac{c_d}{9}
S_1S_2S_3S_4
\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}.
\]
Since
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)
\ge
\int_B\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\ge
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy,
\]
we obtain
\[
R_1R_2R_3R_4
=
\operatorname{Vol}(R)
\ge
\frac{c_d}{9}
S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4.
\]

This completes the proof.
