\title{Original Problem: Uniqueness of Invariant Probability Measures for the Dirichlet SPDE with Drift \(\delta_0\)}
\author{}
\date{}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\section*{Overview / Strategy}

The original target is to decide whether the Markov semigroup \(P_t\) of the
Dirichlet stochastic heat equation with distributional drift \(\delta_0\) has
at most one invariant probability measure.  The first part of the argument
constructs the natural Gibbs measure
\[
\nu(\dd v)=Z^{-1}\exp\!\left(2\int_0^1\1_{\{v(x)>0\}}\dd x\right)\gamma(\dd v),
\]
where \(\gamma\) is Brownian-bridge law, and proves that \(\nu\) is invariant by
passing to the limit from the smooth mollified equations.  This is carried out
in Theorem \ref{thm:invariance}.

Uniqueness requires more than this invariant-measure construction.  The clean
abstract mechanism is that pairwise non-singularity of one transition skeleton
implies uniqueness; see Theorem \ref{thm:pairwise}.  A compact-local Hellinger
overlap estimate for the mollified last-step kernels would imply such
non-singularity in the limit; see Theorem \ref{thm:hellinger}.  A second route is
to prove that every invariant measure is absolutely continuous with respect to
\(\nu\); see Theorem \ref{thm:uac} and Proposition \ref{prop:resolvent}.  The
stated ABLM-type assumptions provide existence, uniqueness in law, and weak
convergence of the mollified equations, but they do not themselves provide any
of these deterministic-start smoothing or overlap estimates.  Thus the
unconditional conclusion proved here is invariance of \(\nu\), together with
precise conditional criteria under which the desired uniqueness follows.

\section{Standing assumptions, notation, and the target statement}

Let
\[
E:=C_0([0,1])
=\{v\in C([0,1],\R):v(0)=v(1)=0\},
\qquad H:=L^2(0,1),
\]
with \(E\) equipped with the supremum norm.  Let
\[
e_k(x):=\sqrt2\sin(k\pi x),\qquad \lambda_k:=\pi^2k^2,\qquad k\ge1,
\]
be the Dirichlet eigenbasis of \(-\partial_{xx}\).  Let \(\gamma\) denote
Brownian-bridge law on \(E\), equivalently the centered Gaussian measure with
covariance \((-\partial_{xx})^{-1}\):
\[
\E_\gamma[(v,e_k)_H(v,e_\ell)_H]=\frac{\delta_{k\ell}}{\lambda_k}.
\]

The SPDE from the original problem is
\begin{align}
\tag{\(*\)}\label{eq:spde}
\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}(x,t)
&=\frac12\frac{\partial^2u}{\partial x^2}(x,t)
+\delta_0(u(x,t))+\dot W(x,t),\qquad t\ge0,\\
u(x,0)&=u_0(x)\in E,\nonumber\\
u(0,t)&=u(1,t)=0.\nonumber
\end{align}

\begin{assumption}[ABLM-type input on the bounded interval]\label{ass:ablm}
For every \(u_0\in E\) and \(T>0\), there is a unique-in-law adapted mild
solution \(u\in C([0,T],E)\) to \eqref{eq:spde}, and the solutions define a
Markov semigroup \((P_t)_{t\ge0}\) on \(E\).  For
\[
b_n(r):=G_{1/n}(r)=\sqrt{\frac n{2\pi}}e^{-nr^2/2},
\]
let \(P_t^n\) be the semigroup of the mollified equation obtained by replacing
\(\delta_0\) with \(b_n\).  For every \(t>0\), \(v\in E\), and \(f\in C_b(E)\),
\[
P_t^n f(v)\longrightarrow P_tf(v),
\]
or equivalently \(P_t^n(v,\cdot)\Rightarrow P_t(v,\cdot)\).
\end{assumption}

Define
\[
F_n(r):=\int_{-\infty}^r b_n(s)\dd s,\qquad
\Phi_n(v):=\int_0^1F_n(v(x))\dd x,\qquad
\Phi(v):=\int_0^1\1_{\{v(x)>0\}}\dd x.
\]
Set
\begin{equation}\label{eq:gibbs}
\nu_n(\dd v):=Z_n^{-1}e^{2\Phi_n(v)}\gamma(\dd v),
\qquad
\nu(\dd v):=Z^{-1}e^{2\Phi(v)}\gamma(\dd v),
\end{equation}
where \(Z_n\) and \(Z\) are the normalizing constants.  Since
\(0\le\Phi_n,\Phi\le1\),
\begin{equation}\label{eq:density-bounds}
1\le Z_n,Z\le e^2,
\qquad
e^{-2}\le \frac{\dd\nu_n}{\dd\gamma},\frac{\dd\nu}{\dd\gamma}\le e^2.
\end{equation}

For a Markov kernel \(K\) and a probability measure \(\mu\), write
\(\mu K(A):=\int_EK(x,A)\mu(\dd x)\).  A probability measure \(\mu\) is invariant
for a semigroup \((P_t)\) if \(\mu P_t=\mu\) for all \(t\ge0\).

\begin{theorem}[Original problem: target and established claim]\label{thm:headline}
The target assertion from the original problem is
\[
\tag{U}\label{eq:target}
\text{\((P_t)\) has at most one invariant probability measure on \(E\).}
\]
Under Assumption \ref{ass:ablm}, the Gibbs measure \(\nu\) in
\eqref{eq:gibbs} is invariant.  Moreover, \eqref{eq:target} follows if one adds
any of the deterministic-start smoothing or overlap hypotheses in Theorem
\ref{thm:hellinger}, Theorem \ref{thm:uac}, or Proposition
\ref{prop:resolvent} together with Proposition \ref{prop:acunique}.  In that
case the unique invariant probability measure is \(\nu\).
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
The invariance statement is Theorem \ref{thm:invariance}.  The Hellinger
overlap hypothesis of Theorem \ref{thm:hellinger} implies pairwise
non-singularity of one skeleton, and Theorem \ref{thm:pairwise} then gives
uniqueness.  The compact-local uniform absolute-continuity hypothesis of
Theorem \ref{thm:uac} gives \(\mu\ll\nu\) for every invariant \(\mu\), and its
third hypothesis, supplied for instance by Proposition \ref{prop:acunique},
then gives \(\mu=\nu\).  Finally, Proposition \ref{prop:resolvent} is a
resolvent version of the same absolute-continuity route.  Since
Theorem \ref{thm:invariance} supplies an invariant measure, any ``at most one''
conclusion identifies the invariant law with \(\nu\).
\end{proof}

\section{The Gibbs invariant measure}

\begin{theorem}[Invariance of the Gibbs measure]\label{thm:invariance}
Under Assumption \ref{ass:ablm}, the measure
\[
\nu(\dd v)
=
Z^{-1}\exp\!\left(2\int_0^1\1_{\{v(x)>0\}}\dd x\right)\gamma(\dd v)
\]
is invariant for \((P_t)\).
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
We prove this by approximation.

\emph{Step 1: \(\nu_n\to\nu\) in total variation.}
For each \(r\ne0\), \(F_n(r)\to\1_{\{r>0\}}\).  Let \(B\sim\gamma\).  For
\(x\in(0,1)\), \(B(x)\) is a nondegenerate centered Gaussian, hence
\(\gamma(B(x)=0)=0\).  Therefore, by Fubini,
\[
\E_\gamma\bigl|\{x\in[0,1]:B(x)=0\}\bigr|
=\int_0^1\gamma(B(x)=0)\dd x=0,
\]
where the endpoints do not matter.  Thus \(|\{x:B(x)=0\}|=0\) for
\(\gamma\)-a.e. \(B\).  For such \(B\),
\[
\Phi_n(B)=\int_0^1F_n(B(x))\dd x
\longrightarrow
\int_0^1\1_{\{B(x)>0\}}\dd x=\Phi(B)
\]
by dominated convergence in \(x\).  Since \(0\le\Phi_n,\Phi\le1\), dominated
convergence in \(\gamma\) gives \(Z_n\to Z\) and
\[
\int_E\left|
Z_n^{-1}e^{2\Phi_n(v)}-Z^{-1}e^{2\Phi(v)}
\right|\gamma(\dd v)\longrightarrow0.
\]
Hence \(\|\nu_n-\nu\|_{\TV}\to0\).

\emph{Step 2: \(\nu_n\) is invariant for \(P_t^n\).}
For fixed \(n\), the drift \(b_n\) is bounded and Lipschitz.  Also
\(\Phi_n\) has \(H\)-gradient \(D\Phi_n(v)=b_n(v)\), because for
\(h\in H\),
\[
D\Phi_n(v)[h]=\int_0^1b_n(v(x))h(x)\dd x=\ip{b_n(v)}{h}_H.
\]
On smooth cylinder functions the formal generator is
\[
L_nF(v)=\frac12\operatorname{Tr}D^2F(v)
-\frac12\ip{-\partial_{xx}v}{DF(v)}_H
+\ip{D\Phi_n(v)}{DF(v)}_H.
\]
The Gaussian integration-by-parts formula for \(\gamma\), together with
\(\dd\nu_n=Z_n^{-1}e^{2\Phi_n}\dd\gamma\), gives for smooth cylinder \(F,G\)
\[
\int_EG\,L_nF\,\dd\nu_n
=
-\frac12\int_E\ip{DG}{DF}_H\dd\nu_n.
\]
Indeed, the derivative of \(e^{2\Phi_n}\) contributes
\(2e^{2\Phi_n}D\Phi_n\), which cancels exactly the added drift term
\(\ip{D\Phi_n}{DF}_H\).  Taking \(G\equiv1\) gives
\[
\int_EL_nF\,\dd\nu_n=0.
\]
For bounded Lipschitz gradient drifts, the standard martingale-problem or
symmetric-Dirichlet-form construction of the stochastic heat equation identifies
\(P_t^n\) with the Markov semigroup generated by the closure of \(L_n\); see,
for example, \cite{DPZ14,MR92}.  Infinitesimal invariance on the cylinder core
therefore implies
\[
\nu_nP_t^n=\nu_n,\qquad t\ge0.
\]

\emph{Step 3: pass to the singular limit.}
Let \(f\in C_b(E)\) and \(t>0\).  By Assumption \ref{ass:ablm},
\(P_t^nf(v)\to P_tf(v)\) for every \(v\in E\).  Using
\(\dd\nu_n/\dd\gamma\le e^2\), dominated convergence gives
\[
\int_E|P_t^nf-P_tf|\dd\nu_n
\le
e^2\int_E|P_t^nf-P_tf|\dd\gamma
\longrightarrow0.
\]
Also, \(\nu_n\to\nu\) in total variation and \(P_tf\) is bounded, so
\[
\int_EP_tf\dd\nu_n\longrightarrow\int_EP_tf\dd\nu,
\qquad
\int_Ef\dd\nu_n\longrightarrow\int_Ef\dd\nu.
\]
Since \(\nu_nP_t^n=\nu_n\),
\[
\int_EP_tf\dd\nu
=
\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_EP_t^nf\dd\nu_n
=
\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_Ef\dd\nu_n
=
\int_Ef\dd\nu.
\]
Thus \(\nu P_t=\nu\) for all \(t>0\), and the case \(t=0\) is trivial.
\end{proof}

\section{Pairwise overlap and Hellinger criteria}

\begin{theorem}[Pairwise non-singularity of one skeleton implies uniqueness]\label{thm:pairwise}
Let \(E\) be Polish and \(K\) a Markov kernel on \(E\).  If
\[
K(x,\cdot)\not\perp K(y,\cdot)\qquad\forall x,y\in E,
\]
then \(K\) has at most one invariant probability measure.  Consequently, if a
semigroup \((P_t)\) has some \(t_0>0\) such that
\(P_{t_0}(x,\cdot)\not\perp P_{t_0}(y,\cdot)\) for all \(x,y\), then
\((P_t)\) has at most one invariant probability measure.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Assume that \(\mu_1\ne\mu_2\) are invariant for \(K\).  Let
\(\sigma:=\mu_1-\mu_2\), and write its Jordan decomposition
\(\sigma=\sigma^+-\sigma^-\).  Since \(\sigma(E)=0\),
\(m:=\sigma^+(E)=\sigma^-(E)>0\).

For every finite signed measure \(\eta\),
\[
|\eta K|\le |\eta|K.
\]
Indeed, for Borel \(A\), taking the supremum over finite measurable partitions
\(\{A_i\}\) of \(A\),
\[
|\eta K|(A)
\le
\sup_{\{A_i\}}\sum_i\int_EK(x,A_i)|\eta|(\dd x)
=
\int_EK(x,A)|\eta|(\dd x).
\]
Since \(\sigma K=\sigma\), we get \(|\sigma|\le|\sigma|K\).  The two measures
have equal total mass, so \(|\sigma|K=|\sigma|\).  Hence
\[
\sigma^+K=\frac12(|\sigma|K+\sigma K)=\sigma^+,
\qquad
\sigma^-K=\frac12(|\sigma|K-\sigma K)=\sigma^-.
\]
Thus \(\alpha:=\sigma^+/m\) and \(\beta:=\sigma^-/m\) are invariant
probabilities, and \(\alpha\perp\beta\).  Choose a Borel set \(A\) with
\(\alpha(A)=1\) and \(\beta(A)=0\).  Invariance gives
\[
1=\int_EK(x,A)\alpha(\dd x),\qquad
0=\int_EK(y,A)\beta(\dd y).
\]
Therefore \(K(x,A)=1\) for \(\alpha\)-a.e. \(x\) and \(K(y,A)=0\) for
\(\beta\)-a.e. \(y\).  Choosing such \(x,y\), \(K(x,\cdot)\) is concentrated on
\(A\) while \(K(y,\cdot)\) is concentrated on \(A^c\), so
\(K(x,\cdot)\perp K(y,\cdot)\), a contradiction.  The semigroup assertion
follows because every invariant measure for \((P_t)\) is invariant for the
skeleton \(P_{t_0}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}[Hellinger affinity]
For probability measures \(\mu,\rho\) on \(E\), define
\[
\Aff(\mu,\rho)
:=
\int_E\sqrt{\frac{\dd\mu}{\dd\lambda}\frac{\dd\rho}{\dd\lambda}}\,\dd\lambda,
\]
where \(\lambda\) is any finite measure dominating both \(\mu\) and \(\rho\).
The value is independent of \(\lambda\).  We use the normalized total variation
norm
\[
\|\mu-\rho\|_{\TV}:=\sup_{A\in\cB(E)}|\mu(A)-\rho(A)|.
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[Elementary Hellinger facts]\label{lem:hellfacts}
For probability measures \(\mu,\rho\),
\[
\mu\perp\rho
\iff
\|\mu-\rho\|_{\TV}=1
\iff
\Aff(\mu,\rho)=0,
\]
and
\[
\|\mu-\rho\|_{\TV}
\le
\sqrt{1-\Aff(\mu,\rho)^2}.
\]
Moreover, if \(\mathcal P\) ranges over finite measurable partitions of \(E\),
then
\[
\Aff(\mu,\rho)
=
\inf_{\mathcal P}\sum_{B\in\mathcal P}\sqrt{\mu(B)\rho(B)}.
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Take \(\lambda=\mu+\rho\), and write \(f=\dd\mu/\dd\lambda\),
\(g=\dd\rho/\dd\lambda\).  The definition is independent of \(\lambda\) because
changing the dominating measure multiplies both densities by the same
Radon--Nikodym factor.  If \(\mu\perp\rho\), then \(fg=0\) \(\lambda\)-a.e., so
\(\Aff(\mu,\rho)=0\).  Conversely, if \(\Aff(\mu,\rho)=0\), then
\(\sqrt{fg}=0\) \(\lambda\)-a.e.; the sets \(\{f>0\}\) and \(\{g>0\}\) separate
\(\mu\) and \(\rho\), so \(\mu\perp\rho\).  The equivalence with
\(\|\mu-\rho\|_{\TV}=1\) is the standard characterization of singularity for
probability measures.

Also,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\|\mu-\rho\|_{\TV}
&=\frac12\int_E|f-g|\dd\lambda                                      \\
&=\frac12\int_E|\sqrt f-\sqrt g|(\sqrt f+\sqrt g)\dd\lambda          \\
&\le \frac12
\left(\int_E(\sqrt f-\sqrt g)^2\dd\lambda\right)^{1/2}
\left(\int_E(\sqrt f+\sqrt g)^2\dd\lambda\right)^{1/2}               \\
&=\sqrt{1-\Aff(\mu,\rho)^2}.
\end{aligned}
\]

For the partition formula, let \(\mathcal P\) be finite.  On each atom \(B\),
\[
\int_B\sqrt{\E_\lambda(f\mid\mathcal P)\E_\lambda(g\mid\mathcal P)}\dd\lambda
=
\sqrt{\mu(B)\rho(B)}.
\]
Conditional Cauchy--Schwarz gives
\[
\E_\lambda(\sqrt{fg}\mid\mathcal P)
\le
\sqrt{\E_\lambda(f\mid\mathcal P)\E_\lambda(g\mid\mathcal P)},
\]
so every partition sum is at least \(\Aff(\mu,\rho)\).  Since \(E\) is Polish,
\(\cB(E)\) is countably generated.  Choosing finite partitions increasing to a
countable generating algebra, the bounded martingale convergence theorem
(applied with \(\lambda=\mu+\rho\), so \(0\le f,g\le1\)) gives partition sums
converging down to \(\int\sqrt{fg}\dd\lambda\).  Hence the infimum equals
\(\Aff(\mu,\rho)\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Mixture lower bound]\label{lem:mixture}
Let \(K\) be a Markov kernel on \(E\).  Suppose \(C\subset E\) is measurable and
there is \(a>0\) such that
\[
\Aff(K(v,\cdot),K(w,\cdot))\ge a\qquad\forall v,w\in C.
\]
Then, for all probability measures \(\alpha,\beta\),
\[
\Aff(\alpha K,\beta K)\ge a\,\alpha(C)\beta(C).
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(\mathcal P\) be a finite partition.  For each atom \(B\),
\[
\sqrt{(\alpha K)(B)(\beta K)(B)}
\ge
\int_C\int_C\sqrt{K(v,B)K(w,B)}\,\alpha(\dd v)\beta(\dd w),
\]
by Cauchy--Schwarz applied to the functions \(\sqrt{K(v,B)}\) and
\(\sqrt{K(w,B)}\).  Summing over \(B\in\mathcal P\) gives
\[
\sum_{B\in\mathcal P}\sqrt{(\alpha K)(B)(\beta K)(B)}
\ge
\int_C\int_C
\sum_{B\in\mathcal P}\sqrt{K(v,B)K(w,B)}
\,\alpha(\dd v)\beta(\dd w).
\]
The inner sum is at least
\(\Aff(K(v,\cdot),K(w,\cdot))\) by Lemma \ref{lem:hellfacts}; hence the last
display is at least \(a\alpha(C)\beta(C)\).  Taking the infimum over
\(\mathcal P\) and using the partition formula proves the claim.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Lower semicontinuity of total variation]\label{lem:tv-lsc}
If \(\mu_n\Rightarrow\mu\) and \(\rho_n\Rightarrow\rho\) weakly on a Polish
space, then
\[
\|\mu-\rho\|_{\TV}
\le
\liminf_{n\to\infty}\|\mu_n-\rho_n\|_{\TV}.
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
For \(\eta=\mu-\rho\), regularity of the finite signed measure \(\eta\) implies
\[
\|\eta\|_{\TV}
=
\sup_{\substack{f\in C_b(E)\\0\le f\le1}}
\left|\int_Ef\dd\eta\right|.
\]
Indeed, indicators of Borel sets are approximated from inside and outside by
continuous functions using regularity of \(|\eta|\) and Urysohn's lemma.  For
each such \(f\),
\[
\int_Ef\dd(\mu_n-\rho_n)\longrightarrow\int_Ef\dd(\mu-\rho),
\]
so
\[
\left|\int_Ef\dd(\mu-\rho)\right|
\le
\liminf_n\|\mu_n-\rho_n\|_{\TV}.
\]
Taking the supremum over \(f\) proves the result.
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}[Conditional uniqueness from compact-local Hellinger overlap]\label{thm:hellinger}
Let \(E\) be Polish.  Let \((P_s)\) and \((P_s^n)\) be Markov semigroups on
\(E\).  Fix \(0<\varepsilon<t\).  Assume:
\begin{enumerate}
\item for every \(x\in E\),
\[
P_t^n(x,\cdot)\Rightarrow P_t(x,\cdot),
\qquad
P_{t-\varepsilon}^n(x,\cdot)\Rightarrow P_{t-\varepsilon}(x,\cdot);
\]
\item for every compact \(C\subset E\), there is \(a(C)>0\) such that
\[
\Aff(P_\varepsilon^n(v,\cdot),P_\varepsilon^n(w,\cdot))\ge a(C)
\]
for all \(n\ge1\) and all \(v,w\in C\).
\end{enumerate}
Then \(P_t(x,\cdot)\not\perp P_t(y,\cdot)\) for all \(x,y\in E\).  Consequently,
if this holds for some \(t>0\), then \((P_s)\) has at most one invariant
probability measure.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Fix \(x,y\in E\), and set
\[
\alpha_n:=P_{t-\varepsilon}^n(x,\cdot),\qquad
\beta_n:=P_{t-\varepsilon}^n(y,\cdot).
\]
The two sequences are tight, because they are weakly convergent.  Hence there
is a compact \(C\subset E\) such that
\(\alpha_n(C)\ge1/2\) and \(\beta_n(C)\ge1/2\) for all \(n\).  By hypothesis 2,
\[
\Aff(P_\varepsilon^n(v,\cdot),P_\varepsilon^n(w,\cdot))\ge a(C),
\qquad v,w\in C.
\]
Using the semigroup property and Lemma \ref{lem:mixture},
\[
\Aff(P_t^n(x,\cdot),P_t^n(y,\cdot))
=
\Aff(\alpha_nP_\varepsilon^n,\beta_nP_\varepsilon^n)
\ge
a(C)\alpha_n(C)\beta_n(C)\ge \frac{a(C)}4.
\]
By Lemma \ref{lem:hellfacts},
\[
\|P_t^n(x,\cdot)-P_t^n(y,\cdot)\|_{\TV}
\le
\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{a(C)}4\right)^2}
=:\rho<1.
\]
Weak convergence at time \(t\), followed by Lemma \ref{lem:tv-lsc}, yields
\[
\|P_t(x,\cdot)-P_t(y,\cdot)\|_{\TV}\le\rho<1.
\]
Thus the two limiting transition probabilities are not singular.  The final
claim follows from Theorem \ref{thm:pairwise}.
\end{proof}

\section{Absolute continuity routes and the Dirichlet-form limitation}

\begin{theorem}[Conditional uniqueness from compact-local uniform absolute continuity]\label{thm:uac}
Let \(E=C_0([0,1])\), and let \(\nu\) be the Gibbs measure in
\eqref{eq:gibbs}.  Fix \(t_0>0\).  Assume:
\begin{enumerate}
\item for every \(x\in E\) and \(f\in C_b(E)\),
\[
P_{t_0}^nf(x)\to P_{t_0}f(x);
\]
\item for every compact \(K\subset E\), the family
\[
\{P_{t_0}^n(x,\cdot): n\ge1,\ x\in K\}
\]
is uniformly absolutely continuous with respect to \(\nu\);
\item every \(P_t\)-invariant probability measure that is absolutely continuous
with respect to \(\nu\) equals \(\nu\).
\end{enumerate}
Then \(\nu\) is the unique invariant probability measure of \((P_t)\).
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Let \(\mu\) be invariant for \((P_t)\), and set
\(\rho_n:=\mu P_{t_0}^n\).  For \(f\in C_b(E)\), dominated convergence and
hypothesis 1 give
\[
\int_Ef\dd\rho_n
=
\int_EP_{t_0}^nf(x)\mu(\dd x)
\longrightarrow
\int_EP_{t_0}f(x)\mu(\dd x)
=
\int_Ef\dd\mu,
\]
so \(\rho_n\Rightarrow\mu\).

Let \(B\) be Borel with \(\nu(B)=0\).  Fix \(\varepsilon,\eta>0\).  By tightness
of \(\mu\), choose compact \(K\) with \(\mu(K^c)<\eta\).  By uniform absolute
continuity on \(K\), choose \(\delta>0\) such that
\[
\nu(A)<\delta
\implies
\sup_{n\ge1}\sup_{x\in K}P_{t_0}^n(x,A)<\varepsilon.
\]
By outer regularity, choose open \(O\supset B\) with \(\nu(O)<\delta\).  Then
for every \(n\),
\[
\rho_n(O)
=
\int_EP_{t_0}^n(x,O)\mu(\dd x)
\le
\varepsilon+\eta.
\]
Since \(O\) is open and \(\rho_n\Rightarrow\mu\), Portmanteau gives
\[
\mu(O)\le\liminf_n\rho_n(O)\le\varepsilon+\eta.
\]
Thus \(\mu(B)\le\varepsilon+\eta\).  Letting \(\varepsilon,\eta\downarrow0\)
shows \(\mu\ll\nu\).  Hypothesis 3 gives \(\mu=\nu\).
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[A simple sufficient condition for uniform absolute continuity]
Hypothesis 2 of Theorem \ref{thm:uac} follows if, for every compact
\(K\subset E\), there are \(p>1\) and \(C_K<\infty\) such that
\(P_{t_0}^n(x,\cdot)=r_{n,x}\nu\) and
\[
\sup_{n\ge1}\sup_{x\in K}\|r_{n,x}\|_{L^p(\nu)}\le C_K.
\]
Indeed, with \(q=p/(p-1)\), Holder's inequality gives
\(P_{t_0}^n(x,A)\le C_K\nu(A)^{1/q}\).
\end{remark}

\begin{proposition}[Pointwise resolvent absolute continuity]\label{prop:resolvent}
Assume that \(\nu\) is invariant and that every invariant probability measure
absolutely continuous with respect to \(\nu\) equals \(\nu\).  If, for some
\(\lambda>0\),
\[
R_\lambda(x,\cdot)
:=
\int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t}P_t(x,\cdot)\dd t
\ll\nu
\qquad\forall x\in E,
\]
then \(\nu\) is the unique invariant probability measure.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(\mu\) be invariant and let \(B\) be Borel with \(\nu(B)=0\).  Then
\(R_\lambda(x,B)=0\) for every \(x\), and therefore
\[
0
=
\int_ER_\lambda(x,B)\mu(\dd x)
=
\int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t}\mu P_t(B)\dd t
=
\int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t}\mu(B)\dd t
=
\frac1\lambda\mu(B).
\]
Thus \(\mu\ll\nu\), and by the assumed uniqueness among \(\nu\)-absolutely
continuous invariant measures, \(\mu=\nu\).
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[What the Gibbs Dirichlet form proves]\label{prop:acunique}
Let \((T_t)\) be a \(\nu\)-symmetric Markov semigroup on \(L^2(\nu)\), and let
\(\mathcal E\) be its closed Dirichlet form.  Assume that the only
zero-energy functions in the domain of \(\mathcal E\) are constants.  Then every
\(T_t\)-invariant probability measure \(\mu\) with \(\mu\ll\nu\) equals \(\nu\).

For the Gibbs measure above, the zero-energy condition holds for the gradient
form
\[
\mathcal E(F,G)=\frac12\int_E\ip{DF}{DG}_H\dd\nu
\]
provided the limiting semigroup is indeed the symmetric semigroup associated
with this form.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(h=\dd\mu/\dd\nu\).  Invariance gives, for bounded \(f\),
\[
\int_ET_tf\,h\dd\nu=\int_Ef\,h\dd\nu.
\]
By symmetry and the \(L^1\)-extension of \(T_t\), this says \(T_th=h\) in
\(L^1(\nu)\).  For \(m\ge1\), let \(h_m=h\wedge m\).  Since
\(r\mapsto r\wedge m\) is concave and \(T_t\) is Markov,
\[
T_th_m\le (T_th)\wedge m=h_m.
\]
Integrating both sides against the invariant measure \(\nu\) gives equality,
hence \(T_th_m=h_m\).  Now \(h_m\in L^2(\nu)\), and a fixed \(L^2\)-function has
zero energy:
\[
\mathcal E(h_m,h_m)
=
\lim_{t\downarrow0}\frac1t\ip{h_m-T_th_m}{h_m}_{L^2(\nu)}
=0.
\]
Thus \(h_m\) is constant.  By monotone convergence,
\(\int h_m\dd\nu\to\int h\dd\nu=1\), so \(h_m\to1\) and \(h=1\) \(\nu\)-a.e.

For the displayed Gibbs gradient form, the density bounds \eqref{eq:density-bounds}
make \(\nu\) equivalent to \(\gamma\) with bounded density above and below.
The Gaussian Poincare inequality for the Brownian-bridge measure,
\[
\operatorname{Var}_\gamma(F)
\le
\frac1{\pi^2}\int_E\|DF\|_H^2\dd\gamma
\]
on cylinder functions, extends by closure.  Hence zero \(\nu\)-energy implies
zero \(\gamma\)-energy and therefore constancy.
\end{proof}

\begin{example}[Irreducibility in \(L^2(\nu)\) does not rule out singular invariant laws]\label{ex:nullmod}
Let \(E=[0,1]\), let \(\nu\) be Lebesgue measure, and define a Markov kernel
\[
K(x,\cdot)=
\begin{cases}
\nu(\cdot),&x\ne0,\\
\delta_0(\cdot),&x=0.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(\nu K=\nu\) because \(\nu(\{0\})=0\), and \(\delta_0K=\delta_0\).  On
\(L^2(\nu)\), however, \(Kf=\int f\dd\nu\) \(\nu\)-a.e.; hence the \(L^2(\nu)\)
operator is symmetric and has only constant fixed functions.  The extra
invariant measure \(\delta_0\) is invisible to the \(L^2(\nu)\) theory because
it is supported on a \(\nu\)-null point.
\end{example}

\section{What is missing from the stated assumptions}

The preceding results identify exactly what would close the uniqueness proof.
One needs a deterministic-start smoothing or overlap estimate, such as:
\[
P_{t_0}(x,\cdot)\not\perp P_{t_0}(y,\cdot)\quad\forall x,y,
\]
or the compact-local Hellinger estimate of Theorem \ref{thm:hellinger}, or
compact-local uniform \(\nu\)-absolute continuity as in Theorem
\ref{thm:uac}, or pointwise resolvent absolute continuity as in Proposition
\ref{prop:resolvent}.  These are not consequences of weak convergence alone.

\begin{example}[Weak convergence can destroy absolute continuity and overlap]
On \(\R\), each Gaussian law \(\mathcal N(0,1/n)\) is absolutely continuous with
respect to Lebesgue measure, but
\[
\mathcal N(0,1/n)\Rightarrow\delta_0,
\]
and \(\delta_0\) is singular with respect to Lebesgue measure.  Likewise,
\(\mathcal N(0,1/n)\) and \(\mathcal N(1,1/n)\) have positive Hellinger affinity
for each \(n\), but their affinities tend to \(0\), and the weak limits
\(\delta_0\) and \(\delta_1\) are singular.  Thus weak convergence of kernels
does not by itself preserve absolute continuity, uniform integrability, or a
positive overlap lower bound.
\end{example}

\begin{remark}[Finite-dimensional domination as a possible missing theorem]
A convenient way to prove the compact-local Hellinger input would be a uniform
finite-dimensional heat-kernel domination theorem.  Let \(P_t^{n,m}\) be the
\(m\)-mode Galerkin approximation of the mollified equation and \(Q_t^m\) the
\(m\)-dimensional Dirichlet Ornstein--Uhlenbeck kernel.  A bound of the form
\[
P_t^{n,m}(a,\cdot)\ge c\,Q_t^m(a,\cdot),
\]
with \(c>0\) independent of \(n,m,a\), together with a passage from Galerkin
kernels to the infinite-dimensional kernels, would yield overlap.  For the
Galerkin potential
\[
\Psi_{n,m}(a)
=
\int_0^1F_n\!\left(\sum_{k=1}^ma_ke_k(x)\right)\dd x,
\]
one has the layer-cake representation, with
\(e(x)=(e_1(x),\ldots,e_m(x))\),
\[
\Psi_{n,m}(a)
=
\int_0^1\int_{\R}
\1_{\{\ip{e(x)}{a}>s\}}\,b_n(s)\dd s\,\dd x.
\]
This displays \(\Psi_{n,m}\) as an average of affine half-space membrane
potentials of total mass \(1\).  Proving the domination estimate is, however,
an additional finite-dimensional heat-kernel theorem; it is not part of
Assumption \ref{ass:ablm}.
\end{remark}

\section*{Final conclusion}

The natural Gibbs measure \(\nu\) is rigorously proved invariant for the
limiting singular semigroup.  The desired uniqueness statement follows from
any of the deterministic-start smoothing or overlap estimates isolated above.
Under only the assumptions explicitly stated in the original problem---namely
well-posedness and weak convergence of the mollified equations---the argument
does not rule out invariant measures singular with respect to \(\nu\).  Thus the
candidate proof establishes invariance and conditional uniqueness, but an
additional smoothing or overlap theorem is needed for an unconditional proof of
\eqref{eq:target}.

\title{A Finite Positive Definite Weighted Tree with a Unique Vertex Satisfying \(w(v)<d_T(v)\) Contains an Irreducible Vertex}
\author{}
\date{}

\begin{document}

\maketitle

\section*{Overview / Strategy}

The proof has two main parts. First, a vertex of weight \(1\) is automatically irreducible, because a decomposition \(u=p+q\) with \(p\cdot q\ge 0\) would force \(1=u^2\ge p^2+q^2\ge 2\). Thus, after this easy case, we may assume that no vertex has weight \(1\).

In that remaining case, let \(b\) be the unique vertex with \(w(b)<d_T(b)\). We prove that \(b\) itself is irreducible. The key input is a rooted-branch estimate for each component of \(T-\{b\}\). That estimate is proved by induction using a Schur-complement calculation and a parity argument for the inverse Gram matrix. Combining the branch estimates with the Schur complement at \(b\), any hypothetical decomposition \(b=x+(b-x)\) with \(x\cdot(b-x)\ge0\) is forced to have \(x=0\) or \(x=b\), contradicting the requirement that both summands be nonzero.

\section*{Statement and notation}

Let \(T\) be a finite tree with vertex set \(V\), edge set \(E\), and weight function \(w:V\to\Z\). We write \(d_T(v)\) for the degree of a vertex \(v\) in \(T\). Let \(L(T)\) be the free abelian group generated by \(V\). We identify a vertex with the corresponding basis element of \(L(T)\). The bilinear form on \(L(T)\) is the symmetric form determined on generators by
\[
u\cdot v=
\begin{cases}
w(u), & u=v,\\
-1, & \{u,v\}\in E,\\
0, & u\ne v \text{ and } \{u,v\}\notin E.
\end{cases}
\]
For \(x\in L(T)\), write \(x^2=x\cdot x\). The form is called positive definite if its extension to \(L(T)\otimes_{\Z}\mathbb R\) is positive definite. Since the Gram matrix has integer entries, if the form is positive definite, then every nonzero \(z\in L(T)\) satisfies \(z^2\in\Z_{>0}\).

A nonzero lattice element \(y\in L(T)\) is called \emph{irreducible} if there do not exist nonzero \(a,c\in L(T)\) such that
\[
y=a+c
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
a\cdot c\ge 0.
\]

\begin{theorem}[Original problem]\label{thm:main}
Let \(T\) be a finite weighted tree with vertex set \(V\), edge set \(E\), and weight function \(w:V\to\Z\). Equip \(L(T)\) with the symmetric bilinear form above. Suppose this form is positive definite, and suppose there is exactly one vertex \(b\in V\) such that
\[
w(b)<d_T(b).
\]
Then \(T\) contains a vertex \(u\) such that \(u\) is irreducible in \(L(T)\).
\end{theorem}

For a connected subgraph or subtree \(C\), write \(d_C(u)\) for the degree of \(u\) inside \(C\). The group \(L(C)\) is the free abelian group on the vertices of \(C\), equipped with the restricted form. If \(x\in L(C)\), write \(x_u\) for the coefficient of the vertex \(u\) in \(x\).

\section*{Auxiliary lemmas}

\begin{lemma}[A weight-one vertex is irreducible]\label{lem:weight-one}
Assume the form on \(L(T)\) is positive definite. If \(u\in V\) satisfies \(w(u)=1\), then \(u\) is irreducible.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Suppose, for contradiction, that
\[
u=p+q
\]
where \(p,q\in L(T)\) are nonzero and \(p\cdot q\ge 0\). Since the form is positive definite and integral, \(p^2,q^2\in\Z_{>0}\). Hence
\[
1=u^2=(p+q)^2=p^2+q^2+2p\cdot q
\ge 1+1+0=2,
\]
which is impossible. Therefore no such decomposition exists, and \(u\) is irreducible.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Schur complement calculation]\label{lem:schur}
Let
\[
M=
\begin{pmatrix}
a & \gamma^T\\
\gamma & B
\end{pmatrix},
\]
where \(B\) is symmetric positive definite. Set
\[
S=a-\gamma^T B^{-1}\gamma.
\]
Then \(M\) is positive definite if and only if \(S>0\). When \(S>0\),
\[
M^{-1}=
\begin{pmatrix}
S^{-1} & -S^{-1}\gamma^T B^{-1}\\
-B^{-1}\gamma S^{-1} &
B^{-1}+B^{-1}\gamma S^{-1}\gamma^T B^{-1}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
We have the factorization
\[
M=
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & \gamma^T B^{-1}\\
0 & I
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
S & 0\\
0 & B
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0\\
B^{-1}\gamma & I
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Indeed, multiplying the last two factors gives
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
S & 0\\
\gamma & B
\end{pmatrix},
\]
and multiplying by the first factor gives
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
S+\gamma^T B^{-1}\gamma & \gamma^T\\
\gamma & B
\end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
a & \gamma^T\\
\gamma & B
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
The first and third factors are transposes of one another and are invertible. Congruence by an invertible matrix preserves positive definiteness, so \(M\) is positive definite exactly when the middle block diagonal matrix is positive definite. Since \(B\) is already positive definite, this is equivalent to \(S>0\).

When \(S>0\), invert the displayed factorization:
\[
M^{-1}=
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0\\
-B^{-1}\gamma & I
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
S^{-1} & 0\\
0 & B^{-1}
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & -\gamma^T B^{-1}\\
0 & I
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Multiplying these three factors gives exactly the stated block formula.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Rooted branch estimate]\label{lem:branch}
Let \(C\) be a finite weighted tree rooted at a vertex \(r\). Let \(A\) be its Gram matrix, and assume \(A\) is positive definite. Suppose
\[
w(r)\ge d_C(r)+1,
\qquad
w(u)\ge d_C(u)\quad\text{for every }u\ne r.
\]
Let \(G=A^{-1}\), and define
\[
\alpha=(A^{-1})_{rr}=e_r^TGe_r,
\]
where \(e_r\) is the coordinate vector of \(r\). Then \(0<\alpha\le 1\), and for every \(x\in L(C)\), if \(c=x_r\), then for every \(k\in\Z\),
\[
(2k-1)c-x^2\le k(k-1)\alpha.
\]
Moreover, if \(k=0\) or \(k=1\), and equality holds with \(x\ne 0\), then \(C\) contains a vertex of weight \(1\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
We first prove a parity estimate for inverse Gram matrices. We claim that, under the hypotheses of the lemma,
\[
0<\alpha\le 1
\]
and that every vector \(\lambda\in\Z^C\) satisfying
\[
\lambda_r\equiv 1\pmod 2,
\qquad
\lambda_u\equiv 0\pmod 2\quad(u\ne r)
\]
also satisfies
\[
\lambda^T G\lambda\ge \alpha.
\]
This is proved by induction on \(|C|\).

If \(C=\{r\}\), then \(A=[w(r)]\). The root hypothesis gives \(w(r)\ge 1\), and positive definiteness also gives \(w(r)>0\). Thus
\[
\alpha=\frac{1}{w(r)}\le 1.
\]
If \(\lambda_r=s\) is odd, then \(s^2\ge 1\), so
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda=\frac{s^2}{w(r)}\ge \frac{1}{w(r)}=\alpha.
\]
This proves the base case.

Assume now that \(|C|>1\). Let \(C_1,\dots,C_m\) be the connected components of \(C-\{r\}\), and let \(r_i\) be the unique vertex of \(C_i\) adjacent to \(r\). Root \(C_i\) at \(r_i\). Let \(A_i\) be the Gram matrix of \(C_i\), set \(G_i=A_i^{-1}\), let \(e_i=e_{r_i}\), and put
\[
\alpha_i=e_i^TG_ie_i.
\]
Each \(A_i\) is positive definite because it is a principal submatrix of \(A\). Also, each rooted tree \(C_i\) satisfies the same type of weight hypotheses: since \(r_i\ne r\),
\[
w(r_i)\ge d_C(r_i)=d_{C_i}(r_i)+1,
\]
and every other vertex \(u\in C_i\) satisfies
\[
w(u)\ge d_C(u)=d_{C_i}(u).
\]
Therefore, by induction,
\[
0<\alpha_i\le 1
\qquad\text{for all }i.
\]

Order the vertices of \(C\) by putting \(r\) first and then the vertices of \(C_1,\dots,C_m\). With respect to this order,
\[
A=
\begin{pmatrix}
w(r) & -\eta^T\\
-\eta & B
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
B=A_1\oplus\cdots\oplus A_m,
\]
where the \(C_i\)-block of \(\eta\) is \(e_i\). By Lemma \ref{lem:schur}, if
\[
S=w(r)-\eta^TB^{-1}\eta,
\]
then \(S>0\) and
\[
\alpha=(A^{-1})_{rr}=\frac{1}{S}.
\]
Since \(B^{-1}=G_1\oplus\cdots\oplus G_m\), we have
\[
\eta^TB^{-1}\eta=\sum_i \alpha_i.
\]
Also \(m=d_C(r)\), so the root hypothesis and the inductive bounds \(\alpha_i\le 1\) imply
\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i
\ge (m+1)-m
=1.
\]
Thus \(0<\alpha=1/S\le 1\).

Now let \(\lambda\in\Z^C\) have odd root coordinate and even nonroot coordinates. In the above ordering, write
\[
\lambda=(s,2\mu_1,\dots,2\mu_m),
\]
where \(s\) is odd and \(\mu_i\in\Z^{C_i}\). Define
\[
\rho_i=e_i^TG_i\mu_i,
\qquad
E_i=\mu_i^TG_i\mu_i,
\qquad
\rho=\sum_i\rho_i.
\]
Using the inverse formula from Lemma \ref{lem:schur} with \(\gamma=-\eta\), we obtain
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
4\sum_i E_i+\frac{(s+2\rho)^2}{S}.
\]
For each \(i\), the vectors \(2\mu_i-e_i\) and \(2\mu_i+e_i\) have odd coordinate at the root \(r_i\) and even coordinates elsewhere in \(C_i\). The induction hypothesis applied to \(C_i\) gives
\[
(2\mu_i-e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i-e_i)\ge \alpha_i,
\qquad
(2\mu_i+e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i+e_i)\ge \alpha_i.
\]
Expanding these two inequalities gives
\[
4E_i-4\rho_i+\alpha_i\ge \alpha_i,
\qquad
4E_i+4\rho_i+\alpha_i\ge \alpha_i.
\]
Hence
\[
E_i\ge |\rho_i|.
\]
Therefore
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
\ge
4\sum_i|\rho_i|+\frac{(s+2\rho)^2}{S}
\ge
4|\rho|+\frac{(s+2\rho)^2}{S}.
\]
Set \(y=2\rho\). Then
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
\ge
2|y|+\frac{(s+y)^2}{S}.
\]
Since \(S\ge 1\), it is enough to prove
\[
(s+y)^2+2S|y|\ge 1.
\]
Let \(a=|s|\ge 1\) and \(u=|y|\). If \(sy\ge 0\), then \(|s+y|=a+u\ge 1\), so the inequality is immediate. If \(sy<0\), then
\[
(s+y)^2+2S|y|
\ge
(a-u)^2+2u
=
\bigl(u-(a-1)\bigr)^2+2a-1
\ge 1.
\]
Thus \(S\lambda^TG\lambda\ge 1\), and hence
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda\ge \frac{1}{S}=\alpha.
\]
This completes the induction and proves the parity estimate.

We now derive the stated branch inequality. Fix \(x\in L(C)\), let \(c=x_r\), and set \(Q=x^2=x^TAx\). For \(k\in\Z\), put
\[
t=2k-1.
\]
Define
\[
\lambda=2Ax-te_r.
\]
Because \(A\) has integer entries and \(x\) has integer coordinates, \(\lambda\in\Z^C\). Its root coordinate is even minus the odd integer \(t\), hence is odd, while every nonroot coordinate is even. The parity estimate therefore gives
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda\ge \alpha.
\]
Using \(G=A^{-1}\), we expand:
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
(2Ax-te_r)^TG(2Ax-te_r)
=
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha.
\]
Hence
\[
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha\ge \alpha,
\]
or equivalently
\[
tc-Q\le \frac{t^2-1}{4}\alpha.
\]
Since \(t=2k-1\),
\[
\frac{t^2-1}{4}
=
\frac{(2k-1)^2-1}{4}
=
k(k-1).
\]
Therefore
\[
(2k-1)c-x^2\le k(k-1)\alpha.
\]

It remains to prove the equality statement for \(k=0,1\). For every \(x\in L(C)\), expanding the form gives
\[
x^2
=
\sum_{\{u,v\}\in E(C)}(x_u-x_v)^2
+
\sum_{u\in C}\bigl(w(u)-d_C(u)\bigr)x_u^2.
\]
Indeed, in the first sum, the term \(x_u^2\) appears once for each edge incident to \(u\), hence \(d_C(u)\) times, and each edge contributes the cross term \(-2x_ux_v\). Therefore the displayed expression equals
\[
\sum_{u\in C}w(u)x_u^2-2\sum_{\{u,v\}\in E(C)}x_ux_v=x^2.
\]
By the hypotheses,
\[
w(r)-d_C(r)\ge 1,
\qquad
w(u)-d_C(u)\ge 0\quad(u\ne r),
\]
so
\[
x^2-c^2
=
\sum_{\{u,v\}\in E(C)}(x_u-x_v)^2
+
\bigl(w(r)-d_C(r)-1\bigr)c^2
+
\sum_{u\ne r}\bigl(w(u)-d_C(u)\bigr)x_u^2
\ge 0.
\]
Thus \(x^2\ge c^2\).

Suppose first that \(k=1\), equality holds in the branch inequality, and \(x\ne 0\). Since \(k(k-1)=0\), equality says
\[
c-x^2=0.
\]
Thus \(x^2=c\). Positive definiteness gives \(x^2>0\), so \(c>0\), and hence \(x^2=|c|\). Since \(x^2\ge c^2\), we get \(|c|\ge c^2\). Because \(c\in\Z\) and \(c\ne 0\), this forces \(c=1\), and then \(x^2=c^2=1\).

Similarly, if \(k=0\), equality says
\[
-c-x^2=0.
\]
Thus \(x^2=-c>0\), so \(c<0\). Again \(x^2=|c|\), and the inequality \(x^2\ge c^2\) forces \(c=-1\) and \(x^2=c^2=1\).

In either case, \(|c|=1\) and \(x^2=c^2\). Therefore equality holds in the nonnegative sum for \(x^2-c^2\), so every summand in that sum is zero. In particular, each edge term \((x_u-x_v)^2\) is zero. Since \(C\) is connected, all coordinates of \(x\) are equal to \(c=\pm 1\).

If \(C\) has only one vertex, then \(C=\{r\}\). The equality \(x^2=c^2\), with \(c^2=1\), gives
\[
(w(r)-d_C(r)-1)c^2=0.
\]
Since \(d_C(r)=0\), we obtain \(w(r)=1\).

If \(C\) has more than one vertex, choose a vertex \(u\ne r\) at maximal distance from \(r\). Such a vertex is a nonroot leaf, so \(d_C(u)=1\). Because all coordinates of \(x\) are \(\pm 1\), we have \(x_u^2=1\). The vanishing of the corresponding nonroot summand gives
\[
\bigl(w(u)-d_C(u)\bigr)x_u^2=0,
\]
hence \(w(u)-d_C(u)=0\), and therefore \(w(u)=1\). Thus, whenever equality holds for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\) with \(x\ne 0\), the branch \(C\) contains a vertex of weight \(1\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Calculation at a vertex]\label{lem:bad-vertex}
Let \(T\) be a finite weighted tree whose form is positive definite, and let \(b\) be a vertex of \(T\). Let \(C\) range over the components of \(T-\{b\}\). For each such component \(C\), let \(r_C\) be the unique vertex of \(C\) adjacent to \(b\), let \(A_C\) be the Gram matrix of \(C\), and define
\[
\alpha_C=(A_C^{-1})_{r_Cr_C}.
\]
Then
\[
\sum_C \alpha_C<w(b).
\]
Moreover, if
\[
x=kb+\sum_C x_C,
\]
where \(k\in\Z\) and \(x_C\in L(C)\), and if \(c_C=(x_C)_{r_C}\), then
\[
x\cdot(b-x)
=
k(1-k)w(b)
+
\sum_C\Bigl((2k-1)c_C-x_C^2\Bigr).
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
If \(T=\{b\}\), then there are no components \(C\), the sum \(\sum_C\alpha_C\) is empty, and the first assertion says \(0<w(b)\), which follows from positive definiteness because \(w(b)=b^2\). The displayed formula also reduces immediately to
\[
(kb)\cdot(b-kb)=k(1-k)w(b).
\]
Thus assume \(T\ne \{b\}\).

Order the vertices of \(T\) by putting \(b\) first, followed by the vertices in the components \(C\) of \(T-\{b\}\). With respect to this order, the full Gram matrix has block form
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
w(b) & -\eta^T\\
-\eta & B
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
B=\bigoplus_C A_C,
\]
where the \(C\)-block of \(\eta\) is \(e_{r_C}\). The matrix \(B\) is positive definite because it is a principal submatrix of the positive definite Gram matrix of \(T\). Applying Lemma \ref{lem:schur}, the Schur complement is positive:
\[
w(b)-\eta^TB^{-1}\eta>0.
\]
Since \(B^{-1}=\bigoplus_C A_C^{-1}\), we have
\[
\eta^TB^{-1}\eta=\sum_C \alpha_C.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_C\alpha_C<w(b).
\]

Now write
\[
x=kb+\sum_C x_C.
\]
Different components of \(T-\{b\}\) are orthogonal to one another, and \(b\) pairs nontrivially with a component \(C\) only at the root \(r_C\), where \(b\cdot r_C=-1\). Hence
\[
b\cdot x=kw(b)-\sum_C c_C.
\]
Also,
\[
x^2
=
k^2w(b)-2k\sum_C c_C+\sum_C x_C^2.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
x\cdot(b-x)
&=x\cdot b-x^2\\
&=
\left(kw(b)-\sum_C c_C\right)
-
\left(k^2w(b)-2k\sum_C c_C+\sum_C x_C^2\right)\\
&=
k(1-k)w(b)
+
\sum_C\Bigl((2k-1)c_C-x_C^2\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
This proves the formula.
\end{proof}

\section*{Proof of the theorem}

\begin{proof}[Proof of Theorem \ref{thm:main}]
Let \(b\) denote the unique vertex of \(T\) satisfying \(w(b)<d_T(b)\). Thus every vertex \(u\ne b\) satisfies
\[
w(u)\ge d_T(u).
\]

If \(T\) contains a vertex of weight \(1\), then Lemma \ref{lem:weight-one} shows that this vertex is irreducible, and the theorem follows. Hence assume from now on that \(T\) contains no vertex of weight \(1\). We will prove that \(b\) is irreducible.

Suppose, for contradiction, that \(b\) is reducible. Then there exists \(x\in L(T)\) such that
\[
x\ne 0,
\qquad
b-x\ne 0,
\qquad
x\cdot(b-x)\ge 0.
\]
Decompose \(x\) according to the components of \(T-\{b\}\):
\[
x=kb+\sum_C x_C,
\]
where \(k\in\Z\), each \(x_C\in L(C)\), and the sum ranges over the components \(C\) of \(T-\{b\}\). Let \(r_C\) be the vertex of \(C\) adjacent to \(b\), and put
\[
c_C=(x_C)_{r_C}.
\]

We now verify that each rooted component \(C\), rooted at \(r_C\), satisfies the hypotheses of Lemma \ref{lem:branch}. Since \(r_C\ne b\), the uniqueness of \(b\) gives
\[
w(r_C)\ge d_T(r_C)=d_C(r_C)+1,
\]
because the only edge incident to \(r_C\) that is not in \(C\) is the edge from \(r_C\) to \(b\). If \(u\in C\) and \(u\ne r_C\), then no edge incident to \(u\) was removed when passing from \(T\) to \(C\), so
\[
w(u)\ge d_T(u)=d_C(u).
\]
Finally, the Gram matrix \(A_C\) of \(C\) is positive definite because it is a principal submatrix of the positive definite Gram matrix of \(T\). Thus Lemma \ref{lem:branch} applies to each component \(C\).

Let
\[
\alpha_C=(A_C^{-1})_{r_Cr_C}.
\]
Applying Lemma \ref{lem:branch} to each component, with the same integer \(k\), gives
\[
(2k-1)c_C-x_C^2\le k(k-1)\alpha_C.
\]
Using Lemma \ref{lem:bad-vertex}, we obtain
\[
\begin{aligned}
x\cdot(b-x)
&=
k(1-k)w(b)
+
\sum_C\Bigl((2k-1)c_C-x_C^2\Bigr)\\
&\le
k(1-k)w(b)+k(k-1)\sum_C\alpha_C\\
&=
k(k-1)\left(\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)\right).
\end{aligned}
\]
By Lemma \ref{lem:bad-vertex},
\[
\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)<0.
\]
If \(k\notin\{0,1\}\), then \(k(k-1)>0\), so the last upper bound is strictly negative. This contradicts \(x\cdot(b-x)\ge 0\). Therefore
\[
k\in\{0,1\}.
\]

For \(k=0\) or \(k=1\), we have \(k(1-k)=0\), and Lemma \ref{lem:branch} gives
\[
(2k-1)c_C-x_C^2\le 0
\qquad\text{for every component }C.
\]
Lemma \ref{lem:bad-vertex} therefore gives
\[
x\cdot(b-x)
=
\sum_C\Bigl((2k-1)c_C-x_C^2\Bigr),
\]
a sum of nonpositive terms. Since \(x\cdot(b-x)\ge 0\), every term in this sum must be equal to \(0\). Hence, for every component \(C\),
\[
(2k-1)c_C-x_C^2=0.
\]
If some \(x_C\ne 0\), then the equality statement in Lemma \ref{lem:branch} implies that \(C\), and therefore \(T\), contains a vertex of weight \(1\). This contradicts our standing assumption that \(T\) has no weight-one vertex. Thus
\[
x_C=0
\qquad\text{for every component }C.
\]
Consequently \(x=kb\). If \(k=0\), then \(x=0\), contradicting \(x\ne 0\). If \(k=1\), then \(b-x=0\), contradicting \(b-x\ne 0\). Both possibilities are impossible.

Therefore no such decomposition of \(b\) exists, and \(b\) is irreducible. Hence \(T\) contains an irreducible vertex.
\end{proof}

\title{Original Problem: Is \(F_w\) Contractible?}
\author{}
\date{}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\section*{Overview / Strategy}

The answer to the original question is negative.  We construct an explicit reducible quasi-reduced word
\[
w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A
\]
in the free group on \(a,b,c,d,e,f\), where capital letters denote inverses, and prove that the associated complex \(F_w\) has nonzero \(H_2(-;\Ftwo)\).  The proof has three main moves.  First, planar topology implies that the subdivided crossing graph of every admissible matching is a forest, and for a forest the admissibility condition is exactly a condition on cyclically consecutive leaves of each tree component.  This is done in Lemmas \ref{lem:forest} and \ref{lem:sidepaths}.

Second, the five uniquely occurring inverse pairs \(bB,cC,dD,eE,fF\) are forced to be isolated uncrossed caps.  Removing these caps leaves a six-point ``skeleton'' with labels \(a,A,a,A,a,A\).  In this skeleton we exhibit three admissible two-cells \(Q_1,Q_2,Q_3\) whose mod-\(2\) cellular boundary cancels.  Finally, the forest condition gives a crossing bound showing that no admissible matching for this word has three or more crossings.  Hence the constructed two-cycle cannot be a boundary, so \(H_2(F_w;\Ftwo)\neq 0\).

\section{Definitions and headline theorem}

Let
\[
G=\langle a_1,\dots,a_r\rangle
\]
be a free group, and let
\[
L=\{a_1,a_1^{-1},\dots,a_r,a_r^{-1}\}.
\]
A word \(w=w_1\cdots w_m\), with \(w_i\in L\), is called \emph{reducible} if its value in \(G\) is the identity.  It is called \emph{quasi-reduced} if it contains no consecutive subword of the form
\[
\ell\,\ell^{-1}\,\ell
\]
for any \(\ell\in L\).

Throughout,
\[
\HH=\RR\times[0,\infty),\qquad \HH^\circ=\RR\times(0,\infty).
\]
For a word \(w=w_1\cdots w_{2n}\), a \emph{matching} for \(w\) is a union \(M\) of \(n\) compact intervals, each properly embedded in \(\HH\), such that:
\begin{itemize}
\item the endpoints of the intervals are exactly
\[
\bd M=\{1,\dots,2n\}\times\{0\};
\]
\item the interiors of the intervals lie in \(\HH^\circ\), any two intervals meet only in finitely many transverse double points, and there are no triple points;
\item for each component \(R\) of \(\HH\setminus M\), and for each component \(A\) of \(M\cap\bd\overline{R}\), the boundary \(\bd A\) consists of two marked boundary points \((i,0)\) and \((j,0)\) with
\[
w_i=w_j^{-1}.
\]
\end{itemize}
A matching is a \emph{\(k\)-crossing matching} if the total number of transverse double points is \(k\).

The CW complex \(F_w\) is the following complex.  Its \(k\)-cells are indexed by isotopy classes, relative to the marked boundary points, of \(k\)-crossing matchings.  If \(M\) is such a matching and \(X(M)\) is its crossing set, the corresponding closed cell is
\[
C(M)=\prod_{c\in X(M)} I_c,
\]
where \(I_c\) is an interval whose two endpoints correspond to the two local resolutions of the crossing \(c\).  If a facet is obtained by fixing the coordinate \(I_c\) at an endpoint \(a\in\bd I_c\), then the facet is attached to the cell corresponding to the matching \(M_a\) obtained from \(M\) by resolving \(c\) according to \(a\).  The remaining coordinates are identified canonically with the crossings of \(M_a\).

\begin{theorem}[Headline theorem: the original question has a negative answer]\label{thm:headline}
There exists a reducible quasi-reduced word \(w\) for which the associated complex \(F_w\) is not contractible.  More precisely, let
\[
G=\langle a,b,c,d,e,f\rangle,
\]
write
\[
A=a^{-1},\quad B=b^{-1},\quad C=c^{-1},\quad
D=d^{-1},\quad E=e^{-1},\quad F=f^{-1},
\]
and set
\[
w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A.
\]
Then \(w\) is reducible and quasi-reduced, but
\[
H_2(F_w;\Ftwo)\neq 0.
\]
Consequently \(F_w\) is not contractible.
\end{theorem}

The proof of Theorem \ref{thm:headline} is completed at the end, after the necessary planar and cellular arguments have been established.

\section{The word}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:word}
The word
\[
w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A
\]
is reducible and quasi-reduced.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
The word freely reduces to the identity.  Indeed, cancelling the adjacent inverse pairs
\[
bB,\qquad cC,\qquad dD,\qquad eE,\qquad fF
\]
leaves
\[
aA\,aA\,aA,
\]
which freely reduces to \(1\).  Thus \(w=1\in G\).

It remains to check that \(w\) is quasi-reduced.  A forbidden subword \(\ell\ell^{-1}\ell\) must begin with an adjacent inverse pair.  The only adjacent inverse pairs in the displayed word, in either orientation, are
\[
bB,\qquad cC,\qquad dD,\qquad eE,\qquad fF.
\]
The letters immediately following these pairs are respectively
\[
A,\qquad a,\qquad A,\qquad a,\qquad A.
\]
These are not respectively
\[
b,\qquad c,\qquad d,\qquad e,\qquad f.
\]
Therefore no adjacent inverse pair begins a subword of the form \(\ell\ell^{-1}\ell\).  Hence \(w\) is quasi-reduced.
\end{proof}

\section{Planar structure of admissible matchings}

We use the Jordan curve theorem and Euler's formula for finite planar graphs in their standard forms; see, for example, \cite{Munkres}.

\begin{definition}
Let \(M\) be a matching.  Its \emph{subdivided crossing graph} \(\Gamma(M)\) is the finite graph whose vertices are the boundary endpoints of the intervals together with the crossing points, and whose edges are the arc segments of the intervals between consecutive vertices.  Boundary endpoints have degree \(1\), and crossing vertices have degree \(4\).
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:forest}
For every admissible matching \(M\), the graph \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Suppose, toward a contradiction, that \(\Gamma(M)\) contains a graph cycle \(\gamma\).  Since boundary endpoints have degree \(1\), the cycle cannot pass through a boundary endpoint.  Hence \(\gamma\) lies in \(\HH^\circ\).  Its image is a simple closed curve.  By the Jordan curve theorem, it bounds a disk \(\Delta\).  The disk \(\Delta\) is contained in \(\HH^\circ\): any point with \(y\leq 0\) can be joined to infinity through the closed lower half-plane without meeting \(\gamma\), so it lies in the unbounded component of the complement of \(\gamma\).

Since \(M\) is a finite union of arcs, \(\Delta\setminus M\) is nonempty.  Let \(U\) be a component of \(\Delta\setminus M\), and let \(R\) be the component of \(\HH\setminus M\) containing \(U\).  Because \(\bd\Delta=\gamma\subset M\), a path in \(R\) cannot cross from \(U\) to the exterior of \(\Delta\).  Thus \(R\subset\Delta\).  In particular \(\bd\overline R\) is disjoint from \(\RR\times\{0\}\).

The set \(M\cap\bd\overline R\) is nonempty, since \(R\) is a bounded complementary component of a nonempty finite graph.  But every component of \(M\cap\bd\overline R\) is disjoint from the boundary line \(\RR\times\{0\}\), and therefore no such component can have boundary equal to two marked boundary points \((i,0)\) and \((j,0)\).  This contradicts admissibility.  Hence \(\Gamma(M)\) has no cycles, so it is a forest.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Side paths in a tree component]\label{lem:sidepaths}
Let \(M\) be a matching such that \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest.  Let \(T\) be a tree component of \(\Gamma(M)\), and let its boundary leaves be
\[
x_1<x_2<\cdots <x_m
\]
in their order along \(\RR\times\{0\}\), with \(x_m\) and \(x_1\) regarded as cyclically consecutive through infinity.  For \(i=1,\dots,m\), with \(x_{m+1}=x_1\), let \(P_i\) be the unique path in \(T\) from \(x_i\) to \(x_{i+1}\).

As \(R\) ranges over the components of \(\HH\setminus M\), the components of \(M\cap\bd\overline R\) lying in \(T\) are exactly the paths
\[
P_1,\dots,P_m.
\]
Consequently, for a forest matching, the admissibility label condition is equivalent to the following condition: for every tree component \(T\), cyclically consecutive boundary leaves of \(T\) have inverse labels.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
First consider only the tree \(T\).  Compactify \(\HH\) by adding the point at infinity to the boundary, obtaining a closed disk \(D\).  The boundary leaves \(x_1,\dots,x_m\) lie on \(\bd D\).  Let \(\alpha_i\) be the boundary arc from \(x_i\) to \(x_{i+1}\), with indices taken cyclically.

Form the planar graph
\[
K=T\cup \bd D.
\]
If \(T\) has \(v\) vertices, then \(T\) has \(v-1\) edges.  The boundary circle \(\bd D\) is cut by the \(m\) leaves into \(m\) boundary edges.  Thus
\[
V(K)=v,\qquad E(K)=v-1+m.
\]
Euler's formula on the sphere gives
\[
F(K)=2-V(K)+E(K)=2-v+(v-1+m)=m+1.
\]
One face is the exterior of the disk \(D\), so \(D\setminus T\) has exactly \(m\) components.

No component of \(D\setminus T\) is disjoint from \(\bd D\).  Indeed, an interior face whose boundary did not meet \(\bd D\) would have a boundary component containing a simple closed curve made of edges of \(T\), contradicting that \(T\) is a tree.  Hence every component of \(D\setminus T\) contains at least one of the arcs \(\alpha_i\).  Since there are \(m\) components and \(m\) such arcs, each component contains exactly one \(\alpha_i\).  Let \(U_i\) be the component containing \(\alpha_i\).

We now show that the part of \(\bd\overline{U_i}\) lying in \(T\) is exactly \(P_i\).  The set
\[
J_i=P_i\cup \alpha_i
\]
is a simple closed curve.  Let \(\Delta_i\subset D\) be the disk bounded by \(J_i\) on the side adjacent to the boundary arc \(\alpha_i\), so that
\[
\Delta_i\cap \bd D=\alpha_i.
\]
We claim that
\[
T\cap \Delta_i=P_i.
\]
If not, some component \(S\) of \(T\setminus P_i\) meets the interior of \(\Delta_i\).  Since \(T\) is a tree, \(S\) attaches to \(P_i\) at exactly one vertex; two distinct attachment vertices would give a cycle in \(T\).  The finite tree \(S\) has a leaf not equal to its attachment point, and because all degree-one vertices of \(T\) are boundary leaves, this leaf is some boundary leaf \(y\neq x_i,x_{i+1}\).  The path in \(S\) from the attachment point to \(y\) starts inside \(\Delta_i\).  To end at \(y\), which is not on \(\alpha_i=\Delta_i\cap\bd D\), it would have to cross the Jordan curve \(J_i\) again.  It cannot cross \(\alpha_i\), because \(T\) meets \(\bd D\) only at its leaves, and it cannot meet \(P_i\) again without creating a cycle in \(T\).  This is impossible.  Therefore \(T\cap\Delta_i=P_i\), and the \(T\)-part of \(\bd\overline{U_i}\) is exactly \(P_i\).  This proves the lemma when the matching consists only of \(T\).

Now restore the other tree components of \(M\).  They are compact and disjoint from \(T\), hence disjoint from each path \(P_i\).  A sufficiently small one-sided collar of \(P_i\) inside the component \(U_i\) therefore avoids all other components of \(M\).  This collar lies in a single component \(R_i\) of \(\HH\setminus M\), and \(P_i\) is a component of \(M\cap\bd\overline{R_i}\).  Conversely, if a component of \(M\cap\bd\overline R\) lies in \(T\), then near any interior point of one of its edges the region \(R\) lies on one side of \(T\), hence inside one of the components \(U_i\) of \(D\setminus T\).  The preceding paragraph shows that the corresponding side of \(T\) is exactly \(P_i\).  Thus the side paths of \(T\) in the full matching are precisely \(P_1,\dots,P_m\).

The final equivalence follows directly.  If the matching is admissible, then every side path \(P_i\) has endpoints with inverse labels, so cyclically consecutive leaves in each tree component have inverse labels.  Conversely, if cyclically consecutive leaves in every tree component have inverse labels, then every component of \(M\cap\bd\overline R\) is one of the paths \(P_i\), and its endpoints satisfy the required inverse-label condition.  Thus the admissibility condition is exactly the stated cyclic leaf condition.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Unique inverse pairs are forced]\label{lem:unique}
Suppose a letter \(x\) and its inverse \(x^{-1}\) each occur exactly once in a word, at boundary points \(p\) and \(q\).  Then in every admissible matching, \(p\) and \(q\) are joined by an isolated uncrossed interval.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(M\) be an admissible matching, and let \(T\) be the tree component of \(\Gamma(M)\) containing \(p\).  By Lemma \ref{lem:sidepaths}, the two side paths of \(T\) incident to \(p\) end at the predecessor and successor of \(p\) among the boundary leaves of \(T\), in cyclic order.  Admissibility forces both of those endpoint labels to be \(x^{-1}\).  Since \(q\) is the unique boundary point with label \(x^{-1}\), both the predecessor and the successor must be \(q\).  This is possible only if \(T\) has exactly two boundary leaves, namely \(p\) and \(q\).

Let \(m\) be the number of boundary leaves of \(T\), and let \(t\) be the number of crossing vertices of \(T\).  Since \(T\) is a tree,
\[
E=(m+t)-1.
\]
On the other hand, boundary leaves have degree \(1\) and crossing vertices have degree \(4\), so the degree sum gives
\[
2E=m+4t.
\]
Combining these equations,
\[
2(m+t-1)=m+4t,
\]
hence
\[
m=2t+2.
\]
Here \(m=2\), so \(t=0\).  Thus \(T\) has no crossing vertices.  A connected component with two boundary leaves and no crossing vertices is a single embedded interval joining those leaves.  Since it is a whole component of \(\Gamma(M)\), it is isolated from every other interval.  Therefore \(p\) and \(q\) are joined by an isolated uncrossed interval.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[Forced caps and the six-point skeleton]\label{prop:forcedcaps}
For the word
\[
w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A,
\]
every admissible matching contains the five isolated uncrossed intervals
\[
(2,3),\qquad (5,6),\qquad (8,9),\qquad (11,12),\qquad (14,15)
\]
in the original numbering.  Deleting these five intervals leaves six boundary points, originally
\[
1,\ 4,\ 7,\ 10,\ 13,\ 16,
\]
which we relabel as \(1,\dots,6\).  Their labels are
\[
a,\ A,\ a,\ A,\ a,\ A.
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
The inverse pairs
\[
b,B;\qquad c,C;\qquad d,D;\qquad e,E;\qquad f,F
\]
each occur exactly once in \(w\).  Lemma \ref{lem:unique} therefore forces the corresponding boundary points to be joined by isolated uncrossed intervals.  In the original numbering these are exactly
\[
(2,3),\qquad (5,6),\qquad (8,9),\qquad (11,12),\qquad (14,15).
\]

Each of these pairs consists of adjacent boundary points.  An uncrossed interval joining adjacent boundary points, together with the boundary segment between them, bounds a disk containing no marked boundary point.  Because the interval is isolated, no other interval can cross it; and because the boundary segment contains no marked boundary point, no other interval can enter that disk without crossing the interval.  Thus each forced interval may be represented, up to isotopy, by a small boundary-parallel cap.

After deleting these five caps, the remaining original boundary points are
\[
1,\ 4,\ 7,\ 10,\ 13,\ 16.
\]
Their labels are respectively
\[
a,\ A,\ a,\ A,\ a,\ A.
\]
Relabelling them \(1,\dots,6\) gives the claimed six-point skeleton.
\end{proof}

\section{A crossing bound}

\begin{lemma}[Crossing count in a forest]\label{lem:crosscount}
Suppose a matching has \(r\) intervals, \(k\) crossings, and \(c\) connected components in its subdivided crossing graph.  If that graph is a forest, then
\[
k=r-c.
\]
In particular, if \(r>0\), then \(k\le r-1\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The graph has
\[
V=2r+k
\]
vertices: \(2r\) boundary leaves and \(k\) crossing vertices.  If an interval has \(s\) crossings, it is subdivided into \(s+1\) edges.  Summing over all intervals gives
\[
E=r+2k,
\]
because each crossing lies on two intervals.  Since the graph is a forest with \(c\) connected components,
\[
E=V-c.
\]
Thus
\[
r+2k=2r+k-c,
\]
so
\[
k=r-c.
\]
If \(r>0\), then \(c\ge 1\), and therefore \(k\le r-1\).
\end{proof}

\section{The two-cells and their facets}

From now on we work in the six-point skeleton of Proposition \ref{prop:forcedcaps}; the five forced caps are always understood to be reinserted afterward.  For skeleton endpoints \(i<j\), write \((ij)\) for an interval joining \(i\) and \(j\).  We use standard chord drawings in the upper half-plane, so two intervals \((i,k)\) and \((j,\ell)\), with \(i<k\) and \(j<\ell\), cross exactly when their endpoints strictly interleave.

\begin{proposition}[Three admissible two-cells]\label{prop:twocells}
Define skeleton matchings
\[
Q_1=(13)(25)(46),\qquad
Q_2=(14)(26)(35),\qquad
Q_3=(15)(24)(36).
\]
After reinserting the five forced caps, each \(Q_i\) is an admissible two-crossing matching for \(w\).  Hence \(Q_1,Q_2,Q_3\) define distinct \(2\)-cells of \(F_w\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
The crossing pairs are
\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
\text{skeleton matching} & \text{crossing pairs}\\
\hline
Q_1 & (13)\times(25),\ (25)\times(46)\\
Q_2 & (14)\times(26),\ (14)\times(35)\\
Q_3 & (15)\times(36),\ (24)\times(36).
\end{array}
\]
Thus each \(Q_i\) has exactly two crossings.  In each case the crossing graph on the three skeleton intervals is connected: for \(Q_1\) the middle interval \((25)\) crosses the other two, for \(Q_2\) the interval \((14)\) crosses the other two, and for \(Q_3\) the interval \((36)\) crosses the other two.

For each \(Q_i\), the subdivided skeleton graph has \(6\) boundary leaves and \(2\) crossing vertices, so
\[
V=8.
\]
It has \(3\) original intervals and \(2\) crossings, hence
\[
E=3+2\cdot 2=7.
\]
Since the graph is connected and \(E=V-1\), it is a tree.

The leaves of this tree are the skeleton endpoints
\[
1,2,3,4,5,6
\]
in cyclic order, with labels
\[
a,A,a,A,a,A.
\]
Cyclically consecutive leaves therefore have inverse labels.  The five forced caps are separate two-leaf tree components, and their endpoint labels are also inverse.  Lemma \ref{lem:sidepaths} now implies that, after reinserting the forced caps, each \(Q_i\) is admissible.  The three matchings have different endpoint pairings, and an isotopy fixing the marked boundary points preserves endpoint pairings.  Hence they define distinct \(2\)-cells.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[One-cells and facet table]\label{prop:facets}
Define six skeleton matchings
\[
\begin{aligned}
P_1&=(12)(35)(46),&
P_2&=(15)(23)(46),&
P_3&=(13)(24)(56),\\
P_4&=(13)(26)(45),&
P_5&=(16)(24)(35),&
P_6&=(15)(26)(34).
\end{aligned}
\]
After reinserting the forced caps, each \(P_i\) is an admissible one-crossing matching for \(w\).  Moreover, over \(\Ftwo\), the cellular boundaries of the two-cells above are
\[
\partial Q_1=P_1+P_2+P_3+P_4,
\]
\[
\partial Q_2=P_1+P_4+P_5+P_6,
\]
\[
\partial Q_3=P_2+P_3+P_5+P_6.
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Each \(P_i\) has exactly one crossing.  The following table records the isolated inverse-labelled interval and the leaves of the crossing component:
\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
P_i & \text{isolated interval} & \text{leaves of crossing component}\\
\hline
P_1 & (12) & 3,4,5,6\\
P_2 & (23) & 1,4,5,6\\
P_3 & (56) & 1,2,3,4\\
P_4 & (45) & 1,2,3,6\\
P_5 & (16) & 2,3,4,5\\
P_6 & (34) & 1,2,5,6.
\end{array}
\]
In every row, the isolated interval has inverse endpoint labels, and the four leaves of the crossing component have alternating labels \(a,A,a,A\) in cyclic order.  The crossing component is a tree with four leaves and one crossing vertex.  Therefore Lemma \ref{lem:sidepaths}, together with the forced caps, shows that each \(P_i\) is admissible.

For a crossing between intervals \((i,k)\) and \((j,\ell)\), where
\[
i<j<k<\ell,
\]
the two local resolutions are
\[
(i,j)(k,\ell)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
(i,\ell)(j,k).
\]
Applying this rule to the crossings of \(Q_1,Q_2,Q_3\) gives:
\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
\text{two-cell} & \text{crossing resolved} & \text{resulting one-cells}\\
\hline
Q_1 & (13)\times(25) & P_1,\ P_2\\
Q_1 & (25)\times(46) & P_3,\ P_4\\
\hline
Q_2 & (14)\times(26) & P_1,\ P_5\\
Q_2 & (14)\times(35) & P_4,\ P_6\\
\hline
Q_3 & (15)\times(36) & P_3,\ P_5\\
Q_3 & (24)\times(36) & P_2,\ P_6.
\end{array}
\]
For example, resolving \((13)\times(25)\) in \(Q_1\) gives either
\[
(12)(35)(46)=P_1
\]
or
\[
(15)(23)(46)=P_2.
\]
The other entries follow by the same resolution rule.

In the cellular chain complex over \(\Ftwo\), orientation signs disappear.  Each facet of a square \(C(Q_i)\) maps canonically and homeomorphically to the one-cell obtained by the corresponding resolution.  Therefore the cellular boundaries are exactly the sums of the four one-cells appearing in the two rows for that \(Q_i\), which are the three displayed formulas.
\end{proof}

\section{No three-cells and nonzero homology}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:nohigh}
The complex \(F_w\) has no cells of dimension \(3\) or higher.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(M\) be any admissible matching for \(w\).  By Proposition \ref{prop:forcedcaps}, \(M\) contains the five forced isolated uncrossed caps
\[
(2,3),\qquad (5,6),\qquad (8,9),\qquad (11,12),\qquad (14,15)
\]
in the original numbering.  These caps have no crossings.

After deleting them, exactly three intervals remain.  Let \(k\) be the number of crossings among these three intervals.  By Lemma \ref{lem:forest}, \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest.  Deleting isolated tree components preserves the property of being a forest, so the subdivided crossing graph of the remaining three intervals is also a forest.  If this remaining forest has \(c\) connected components, Lemma \ref{lem:crosscount} gives
\[
k=3-c\le 2.
\]
Thus every admissible matching for \(w\) has at most two crossings.  Since \(k\)-cells of \(F_w\) correspond to \(k\)-crossing matchings, \(F_w\) has no cells in dimensions \(3\) or higher.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:homology}
For the word \(w\) above,
\[
H_2(F_w;\Ftwo)\neq 0.
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Work in cellular chains over \(\Ftwo\), and write the same symbol for a cell and for its corresponding cellular generator.  By Proposition \ref{prop:facets},
\[
\partial Q_1=P_1+P_2+P_3+P_4,
\]
\[
\partial Q_2=P_1+P_4+P_5+P_6,
\]
\[
\partial Q_3=P_2+P_3+P_5+P_6.
\]
Adding these three equations over \(\Ftwo\), each \(P_i\) appears exactly twice.  Hence
\[
\partial(Q_1+Q_2+Q_3)=0.
\]
Thus
\[
z\defeq Q_1+Q_2+Q_3
\]
is a cellular \(2\)-cycle over \(\Ftwo\).

The chain \(z\) is nonzero.  Indeed, cellular chain groups are free \(\Ftwo\)-vector spaces on the cells, and \(Q_1,Q_2,Q_3\) are distinct \(2\)-cells by Proposition \ref{prop:twocells}.  By Proposition \ref{prop:nohigh}, there are no \(3\)-cells, so
\[
C_3(F_w;\Ftwo)=0.
\]
Therefore
\[
B_2(F_w;\Ftwo)=\operatorname{im}\bigl(\partial:C_3(F_w;\Ftwo)\to C_2(F_w;\Ftwo)\bigr)=0.
\]
Since \(z\neq 0\) and \(z\) is a cycle, it represents a nonzero cellular homology class.  By the cellular homology theorem \cite[Chapter 2]{Hatcher}, cellular homology agrees with ordinary singular homology for CW complexes.  Therefore
\[
H_2(F_w;\Ftwo)\neq 0.
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}[Proof of Theorem \ref{thm:headline}]
Proposition \ref{prop:word} shows that the displayed word \(w\) is reducible and quasi-reduced.  Proposition \ref{prop:homology} shows that
\[
H_2(F_w;\Ftwo)\neq 0.
\]
A contractible space has trivial homology in every positive degree over every coefficient field.  Hence \(F_w\) is not contractible.  This gives a negative answer to the original question.
\end{proof}
